column1: Text

With the surge in automotive popularity during the early 20th century, largely thanks to Henry Ford's iconic Model T, cars have become an ever-present fixture in our lives. Now, a growing chorus of voices is questioning our reliance on these vehicles, and I, for one, believe that scaling back our car usage could be a beneficial endeavor.

Elizabeth Rosenthal, in her compelling piece "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," highlights a critical issue. Cars, particularly prevalent in middle-class suburban settings like those in Shanghai or Chicago, pose a significant obstacle to reducing vehicle emissions, according to expert analysis. While cars contribute to 12% of Europe's pollution, this figure soars to as high as 50% in the United States, where car-dependent lifestyles are more ingrained! Robert Duffer's article, "Paris bans driving due to smog," vividly illustrates the detrimental environmental impact of cars. The extreme measure of banning vehicles based on license plate numbers underscores their role in exacerbating urban pollution.

Andrew Selsky's article, "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota," showcases an inspiring alternative. Millions in Colombia embraced a car-free day, opting for walking, cycling, or public transportation. The city streets transformed into tranquil spaces! This annual event, banning private cars, allows buses and taxis to navigate the metropolis of seven million residents. These initiatives foster cleaner air and have catalyzed urban renewal in the Colombian capital, with new parks, sports facilities, pedestrian walkways, and flourishing commercial activity.

In conclusion, the evidence suggests that reducing car dependence and embracing car-free days yields tangible benefits, including cleaner air and urban revitalization. The Colombian experience demonstrates the potential for traffic alleviation and infrastructure improvements. Adopting a similar approach in America, perhaps through increased reliance on cycling or walking for shorter trips, could offer a pathway towards a more sustainable future. I am of the opinion that reducing our dependency on cars is a decision that's actually pretty smart.

Most countries need transportation, it's a global thing. Cars, buses, everything gets us around faster, which is great. But, all this driving? It's a big pollution problem. We use cars every single day, but it’s also a source of greenhouse gases, and cutting back could really help.

There's this little German town called Vauban, trying out a "no car" life. Stores and stuff are super close to homes, not way out on some highway. People can get around easier. Sure, there are still cars there, but get this – 70% of the families? They don't even own one! A bit strange, but 57% of the families there said that they actually sold their cars just to move there. Some Vauban folks even say they feel less stressed without driving so much. Now, cars cause about 12% of global greenhouse gas emissions. But get this: in places where everyone drives, like parts of the United States, that number can jump all the way up to 50%.

Then there’s Paris. When smog was a really big issue, they tried something pretty cool to get rid of it. On Mondays, cars with license plates that ended with an even number weren't allowed to drive. No exceptions. It was either that or a fine. On Tuesdays, they switched it: odd-numbered plates were banned. Guess what? Traffic went down by 60% in just five days! See, diesel fuel was a big part of the smog problem there, since most cars in France ran on it. But when they stopped the odd-number ban on Tuesdays, it kinda messed up how well the plan worked.

Can you picture a whole day with no cars at all? It actually happens once a year in Bogota, Colombia. Nobody drives their private cars, except for buses and taxis. Even a single day of no cars can make a difference in pollution. This event has been going on since the 90s. Since then, they built all sorts of new bike paths and sidewalks, plus new parks and centers have popped up. Living without a car can be hard, sure, but it's also a really good thing.

Basically, getting rid of all cars probably isn't gonna happen anytime soon. But countries should be pushing to reduce how much we drive and how much we pollute. If we all work together to cut down on air pollution, it would have a big effect on the world. We rely on cars and transportation, but overusing them can really hurt the planet, and our health too.

Elisabeth Rosenthal proposes that America's infatuation with automobiles might be waning. Her core argument is that car usage is on a downward trajectory, which surprisingly, might be advantageous. A decrease in driving could translate to enhanced safety, improved health, and reduced pollution, leading to a diminished reliance on cars.

Firstly, a decline in driving has the potential to make us both safer and healthier. Personal safety is paramount. Andrew Selsky recounted the eerie emptiness of streets during a car-free day in Colombia. Avoiding traffic congestion promotes tranquility. Traffic can disrupt schedules and induce stress when commuting. Reduced driving could alleviate both traffic and stress. Heidrun Walter shared that shedding car ownership led to a significant reduction in personal tension and an increase in happiness. Moreover, less driving encourages physical activity. While cars facilitate transportation, busy lifestyles often hinder recreational pursuits. Therefore, curtailing driving can foster increased activity levels. Selsky noted the proliferation of parks and sports centers throughout the city. Fewer cars mean more green spaces, while more parks and sports centers equate to more opportunities for exercise. Therefore, less driving means less stress and better health.

Secondly, curbing driving benefits the planet. Although environmental concerns may not resonate with everyone, they are undoubtedly crucial. Pollution is detrimental to both the environment and human health, and cars are significant contributors to pollution. Rosenthal argues that reduced driving "will have beneficial implications for carbon emissions and the environment." Less driving means less pollution. Reduced driving offers a chance to improve the world and have a better future. Pollution is a serious issue and drivers are somewhat responsible.

Also, our reliance on cars would diminish. Many consider cars indispensable, failing to recognize their dependence on them. Technological advancements have reshaped our lives, causing a disconnection from nature. Dependence is not always positive. David Goldberg states that "all our development since World War II has been centered on the car, and that will have to change." Although opinions may differ, depending on a potentially harmful entity may not constitute progress. Rosenthal observed that "cash-strapped Americans could not afford new cars, and the unemployed weren't going to work anyway." Car ownership is not universally accessible. Car-dependent individuals have distanced themselves from nature and often neglect environmental considerations. Less driving could offer solutions to these problems.

In essence, reducing driving could foster improved health, mitigate pollution, and lessen car dependence. Driving less cultivates a cleaner, more structured world, ensuring human well-being and security. Perhaps America's progress stems from reduced driving, emphasizing collective welfare.

Are you often driving? Do you use your car to go to work or the store? Have you considered how many others drive, even for short trips? It is easy to ignore the environmental impact of each car ride. Could you avoid driving today? Many people think, "I need my car!" However, reducing car use is doable. Cities like Havana, Paris, and Bogotá have decreased driving successfully. Driving less offers many benefits for you and others.

Surprisingly, driving less improves your mood. Heidrun Walter noted that someone was less stressed without a car. Think about road rage. It is bad for your health. Driving requires focus – navigating routes, handling rude drivers – leading to stress. Big cities such as New York and Paris often have traffic and pollution. Robert Duffer mentioned in "Paris bans driving due to smog" that car fumes get trapped by warm air, which is not healthy. Duffer also said Paris banned cars briefly due to severe pollution, quickly cleaning the air. People then walk, bike, or skate, providing exercise and improving mental health compared to sitting in a car.

Reducing cars also beautifies the city. Pollution harms plants and smells bad, darkening the environment. Less driving means fewer fumes and a cleaner city. Elisabeth Rosenthal wrote in "In German Suburb, Life goes on Without Cars" that fewer cars increase foot traffic, leading to more sidewalks and shops. Andrew Selsky added that parks, sports centers, smooth sidewalks, new restaurants, and fancy stores appeared. Environmental Protection Agency advocates for "car-reduced" areas, as Rosenthal stated, leading to less pollution. Bogotá, Colombia, has an annual "car-free" day, fining other drivers. Although fining drivers is debatable, it is effective, reducing smog. Therefore, reducing driving offers benefits like improved health, better cities, and a cleaner environment.

Automobiles? Rather incredible! Perhaps a significant advancement, truthfully. They transport individuals from location to location. Does this not fulfill its core purpose? Expedited travel is desired, and cars provide that! Distances diminish quickly—few alternatives compare. Certainly, trains and buses offer alternatives, yet cars possess a perceived appeal. However, car usage precipitates considerable anxiety and environmental impact. Buses and trains negate automobile maintenance and congested roadways, thereby diminishing air contamination! Diminished vehicular usage produces positive effects.

This benefits both people and the environment. A decline in air pollution is important. Vehicles emit unfavorable emissions—greenhouse gases—through exhaust. Individual vehicles possess limited impact; however, large volumes of cars creates problems, most notably in densely populated areas like Paris. Severe pollution events led to vehicular restrictions. Robert Duffer’s "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," states that registration numbers ending in even values faced driving restrictions with monetary penalties. Subsequently, regulations shift to odd numbers! This lessened vehicular traffic by 60%! Andrew Selsky in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota" says that Bogota, Colombia, implements vehicle-free days to combat air pollution, to inspire alternative forms of transport and environmental betterment. The global community acknowledges how car limitations assist environmental protection.

Consider stress reduction! Driving generates anxiety. Gridlock, consistent maintenance, and commotion escalate strain. Reduced driving translates to less stress! Freedom from gridlock, repairs, and noisy conditions. Elisabeth Rosenthal quotes Hartmut Walter in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," who stated "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." He conveys that vehicular abandonment resulted in increased contentment and decreased stress. According to Andrew Selsky again in "Carfree Day Is Spinning into a Big Hit in Bogota," vehicle-free days present "a good opportunity to take away stress..." Selecting tranquility offers value. Diminished stress promotes enhanced existence.

Decreasing vehicular use supports environmental and individual welfare. The environment demands essential support. Furthermore, stress results in detrimental consequences. Achieving an optimal environment and minimizing stress through this action becomes viable. While initial comprehension may lack, implementation in numerous locales demonstrates efficacy. This mechanism produces advantageous outcomes.

Ugh, the electoral college? So messed up! We don't get to just pick our president. Nope, we pick electors, and *they* pick for us, no matter if they are a blue donkey or a red elephant. But hold on, source two says those electors can just ignore what we want! How is that even democracy? We vote for someone to have our backs and make things better, but this whole thing ruins it. The big issue? "Winner-take-all" is a terrible rule.

We don't pick our leader directly, not right now. Like source two says, we vote for electors; each state gets a bunch. Then *they* decide who wins. Source two says 538 electors are out there, and you need 270 to win. A state's electors? Easy, it’s how many people they have in Congress. Want a fair system? Every vote needs to count! No electoral college would mean every single vote adds up. Candidates would get votes based on what people actually want, and ties? Rare. Right now, if most people in your state like someone else, your vote is useless. Ditch the college, and boom, your vote matters!

Source two talks about "faithless" electors. These guys could vote for the other team! That's a slap in the face and dangerous for America. If a Republican elector goes Democrat, bam! Republican votes are trash. We only get to pick a president every four years. Electors changing their vote? Absolutely not okay.

Sources two and three keep bringing up the "winner-take-all" thing. Lose, and your votes are just garbage. The winner gets everything! It's like those voters who picked the loser secretly wanted the winner.

So, state senators, toss the electoral college in the trash! It's awful. It makes some votes worth more than others. It lets electors pick based on their own feelings. And that "winner-take-all" thing? It just throws away votes for the losers.

Dear Senator, I am writing to you today to request a reevaluation of the way in which we choose our nation's leader. It's quite important that we ditch the Electoral College in favor of a system which better represents what We the People actually want. As it stands, this current setup is simply unfair – electors have the potential to completely overturn the will of the American populace!

Now, I know, changing the Electoral College might sound like a bad idea. However, the benefits of doing so might well outweigh any possible downsides. A direct vote might, for example, tempt candidates to start making promises to buy votes. Also, thinking about how to handle ties and keep things fair in a direct vote system is essential. Thus, while scrapping the Electoral College entirely might not be the best approach, fixing it in some way is a must.

At the moment, those electors hold far too much power in deciding who becomes president. This "winner-takes-all" approach we use in the Electoral College actively hurts voters, since candidates only pay attention to a handful of "swing" states. Back in 2000, for example, they didn't even bother with seventeen states! Because of issues like these, it's clear the Electoral College has aged poorly and should be either changed or completely replaced.

Candidates select electors to represent them during their campaigns. These electors then assist them in drumming up votes, all without the candidate needing to visit every state in person. Basically, the presidential hopeful just sits and waits for the results to come in. The candidate could even win the election without campaigning much, as it's ultimately the electors who decide. This leaves the door open for candidates to try bribing electors in key states in order to secure their vote.

When people go to vote, they're technically voting for electors, not the candidates themselves. The electors are chosen in different ways, like at state meetings or directly by the candidates. One major concern with the Electoral College is what I'd call the "disaster factor." That is, it allows for bad things to occur, such as in 1960, when folks in Louisiana nearly replaced Democratic electors with ones who opposed John F. Kennedy.

All in all, the Electoral College is an outdated method of conducting elections. It puts power in the hands of electors instead of the people. Back when society wasn't as educated, having representatives pick the president may have made sense, but it simply doesn't fit with our modern democracy today.

Alright, folks, listen close! We see vehicles everywhere. Sure, they're handy, but picture this: what if we didn't use them as much? Think about it – better air quality and more money staying with you. Seriously, cutting back on car use is a smart choice.

Check out Paris, for example. Robert Duffer mentioned: "Paris bans driving due to smog." They came up with a smart solution. One day odd license plates could drive and even plates the next. And if you broke the rules, you paid a fine. They even offered free public transport for a week! Traffic and smog? Gone! The air cleared up, and people saved money getting around. Duffer reported congestion dropped 60%! He said, "Congestion was down 60 percent in the capital of France, after five days of intensifying smog...rivaled Beijing, China, which is known as one of the most polluted cities in the world". The smog was terrible, but less cars had a HUGE impact.

Consider Vauban, Germany, too. Elisabeth Rosenthal penned "In German Suburb, life goes on without cars." They went all-in, basically ditching cars altogether. Around 70% don't even own one, since parking is pricey outside the town and cars are generally banned there. They absolutely LOVE it. Heidrun Walter explained "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way". Fewer young people even care to get licenses anymore! As Rosenthal put it in "The end of car culture," cars are simply tools for getting from one place to another now. Nobody's too concerned about the car itself, just getting around easily.

The main point? Cars take you places, but so do buses, trains, bikes, and carpools. Some are even free! And less pollution and traffic? It's a win-win for our health and the planet.

Plumer (Source 2) declares the Electoral College is undeniably unfair, antiquated, and illogical. Many agree, seeing it as either a potential catastrophe or simply flawed. While some defend it for preventing runoffs or empowering larger states, most find these justifications unconvincing. Critics of the Electoral College strongly advocate for a straightforward popular vote, deeming it a superior method.

A major flaw lies in the unequal weighting of votes. Electors wield more influence than individual citizens. Direct voting would ensure that every single vote carries equal significance. Furthermore, the potential for electors to be influenced or bribed undermines the democratic process entirely.

Numerous individuals believe the Electoral College is obsolete. Plumer (Source 2) references Bob Dole's call to "Abolish the Electoral College!" A significant number concur, favoring direct voting over decisions made by a select group. Some even quip that a toddler randomly throwing darts could achieve better results. According to Plumer (Source 2), "Over 60 percent of voters desire a direct election," a sentiment that has grown since 2000.

Frankly, the Electoral College is peculiar. Electors, not the general public, directly choose the president, resulting in confusion. Plumer (Source 2) explains, "You vote for electors, who then vote for the president. Who are these electors? Typically, individuals not holding public office. Who selects them? This varies by state – a party committee, perhaps, or the candidate. Can voters control their electors' votes? Not always! Do voters mistakenly vote for incorrect electors? Occasionally!" This intricate system raises questions about who truly holds power.

While the Electoral College may offer some benefits, such as maintaining stability, the majority believes its disadvantages outweigh them. A simple popular vote appears considerably fairer. As Plumer (Source 2) succinctly puts it, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational!"

Established long ago by the country's founders and enshrined in our Constitution, the Electoral College has been in place for quite some time. I believe we should maintain this system since it has functioned reasonably well up until now, and there isn't a valid justification for eliminating it. The Electoral College's continued existence is logical because it is beneficial in approximately five crucial scenarios.

The Electoral College does present some risks. Electors might stray from their party allegiance, casting votes unexpectedly. Essentially, people vote for electors who subsequently choose the president. These electors could disregard the popular vote and vote according to their own preferences. Moreover, a state could potentially submit two different slates of electors, leading to chaos. While a tie vote is extremely improbable in a direct popular vote scenario, the Electoral College always carries this risk. In certain states, candidates frequently neglect campaigning, as one party maintains a decisive advantage, exacerbated by the "winner-takes-all" principle. In a direct popular vote system, candidates might intensify their efforts in every state to garner additional votes. However, a substantial portion of the arguments against the Electoral College center on unusual circumstances; therefore, examining the opposing viewpoint is also prudent.

Swing states effectively determine the outcome of presidential elections, thus voters there are typically well-informed. Recognizing the significance of their votes, they closely monitor news and candidate activities. Candidates devote considerable time and resources to these states, endeavoring to secure their support. Numerous other states are virtually predetermined before the election due to the dominance of one party. Voters in swing states probably contemplate their preferences and values more carefully, making them arguably the most qualified individuals to elect the country's leader.

The Electoral College mitigates the necessity for supplementary elections, thereby reducing confusion. Without the Electoral College, a direct vote system with exceptionally close results could engender considerable challenges. For instance, Nixon's victory in 1968 and Clinton's in 1992, despite securing only around 43% of the vote, underscores the relevance of the Electoral College.

I understand why some voters in states with a strong partisan leaning may dislike the Electoral College. They might exhibit reduced engagement in campaigns, presuming their votes won't alter the state's outcome. However, one individual's vote is inconsequential, and not all citizens participated in the 2012 election. For the majority, expressing their preferred candidate outweighs influencing the entire election. Engaging with governmental processes enhances the likelihood that your vote will have genuine significance.

Senator,

I really want you to keep the Electoral College! This system has 538 electors. To win the White House, a candidate must secure more than 270 votes. Each state chooses electors, usually tied to the candidate's political party. Keeping the Electoral College is very important; it ensures a clear win and a president representing everyone.

A big reason to keep the Electoral College is that it almost always gives one candidate a clear win. It's technically possible to tie, but very unlikely, even with 538 electors being an even number. In 2012, Obama got about 62% of the electoral votes but only about 51% of the popular vote. Most states give all their electoral votes to the state winner. Thus, even a small lead can mean a big electoral vote win. Still, this "winner-take-all" method means candidates mostly focus on states where the race is close, ignoring others. Yet, the winner usually gets a bigger share of Electoral College votes than overall votes.

Another key reason is that the president must represent all citizens. The Electoral College makes candidates win votes from everywhere. No single area has enough electoral votes to choose a president alone. A candidate very popular in one region still needs to campaign in other states; more votes in their safe states won't get them more electoral votes. A president without support across the country would not be a good leader. People in other regions might feel their votes don't matter or the president does not represent them.

In short, keeping the Electoral College is a must because it avoids ties and ensures the president represents every American.

Heidrun Walter mentioned feeling perennially stressed while owning a car and now revels in the freedom of a car-free life. This sentiment reflects a burgeoning trend of people moving away from car dependence. While cars were once essential for mobility and everyday life, declining car sales and reduced driving habits indicate a shift towards recognizing the merits of decreased car usage. A major advantage is the potential to mitigate the greenhouse effect, alleviate smog and air pollution, and stimulate diverse sectors of the economy beyond automotive and transportation industries.

Crucially, the greenhouse effect, significantly fueled by car emissions, poses a threat to environments, ecosystems, and our very existence. The article "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars" reports that cars contribute 12% of Europe's greenhouse gas emissions, and around 50% in some US regions. Reducing our reliance on cars could lead to decreased emissions, ultimately benefiting life on Earth. Such a reduction could positively impact global issues like global warming and rising sea levels. Ergo, reducing car use is beneficial due to its potential to lower greenhouse gas emissions.

Another critical issue is smog and air pollution, which detrimentally affect both human health and the environment. Robert Duffer's article, "Paris Bans Driving Due to Smog," states that "After Days of near-record pollution, Paris enforced a partial driving ban to clear the air of the global city." This five-day ban led to improvements in air quality. Implementing such restrictions regularly could lead to reduced pollution and smog. It could also encourage the adoption of alternative transportation modes like public transit, bicycles, and walking. "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" showcases how limiting car use in a capital city can result in more bike paths, parks, sports centers, and improved sidewalks, alongside rush-hour traffic regulations. Thus, decreasing car use can not only decrease smog and air pollution but also foster a more vibrant city environment.

Finally, curbing car use can foster economic growth in various sectors. According to one source, a significant portion of US transportation funds have historically been allocated to highways. Shifting away from car dependence could free up resources for essential areas like education, government programs, and community development. Moreover, reducing car use can facilitate resource conservation, empowering individuals to invest in other facets of their lives, as noted by another source. The declining driving rates further illustrate this trend. One source observes that "Demographic shifts in the driving population suggest that the trend may accelerate. There has been a large drop in the percentage of 16 to 39yearolds getting a license." This shift allows us to concentrate on economic revitalization. Plainly, decreased car use offers economic advantages, including increased flexibility in government budgets, cost savings, and economic recovery.

In conclusion, curtailing car use offers numerous benefits. These limitations can bolster the economy, lower greenhouse gas emissions, and mitigate smog and air pollution in densely populated regions.

Selecting a presidential candidate carries immense weight, as their actions will inevitably reshape the nation, whether positively or negatively. The electoral college seeks to represent the preferences of each state, despite ongoing debates regarding its effectiveness. Personally, I believe it offers a reasonable reflection of the collective will within each state, thereby indicating the general sentiment of the entire country.

Although some individuals criticize the electoral college for its perceived inability to select the most qualified president and deem it unjust, it's essential to remember that we retain the power to express our preferences! Our voices still hold considerable sway in shaping outcomes. Fundamentally, every vote counts, rendering it far superior to abstaining altogether. While improvements can always be pursued, perfection remains elusive. Universal satisfaction is unattainable. Consequently, I hold the view that the electoral college effectively aggregates the votes.

Despite its intricacies, devising a superior alternative proves challenging. Predominantly, concerns arise regarding the electoral college's ability to accurately reflect our collective desires. However, I dispute this notion. When casting your ballot, you're endeavoring to garner support for your preferred candidate. Your vote contributes to steering the electoral college in your desired direction. This fosters unity among the states by ensuring that the majority vote reflects the desires of each state, which, in my estimation, is quite commendable.

Certain critics argue that the electoral college grants disproportionate influence to larger states. While this may be true to some extent, perhaps it is justifiable. States with larger populations naturally receive more votes. Nonetheless, each vote carries equal weight, preventing those states from gaining an excessive advantage. Even if some individuals disapprove, the electoral college provides a reasonably equitable means of gauging the overall sentiment of the country, which is to be expected.

Ultimately, the electoral college effectively represents the desires of each state by utilizing the majority vote to determine the presidential victor. While not flawless, a direct popular vote system would likely engender even greater challenges. Every system possesses flaws, yet the electoral college offers a relatively organized means of tallying votes over time. Despite not garnering universal approval, universal satisfaction remains an unattainable ideal.

Dear Senator, I've been pondering the optimal method for electing the President and their administration, and I've arrived at a conclusion. We have two established approaches: the Electoral College and a simple tally of all votes cast. After careful consideration, I contend that one method is demonstrably superior.

The Electoral College involves electors convening to vote for the President and Vice President. These 538 individuals ultimately determine the election's outcome. Frankly, I find this system flawed. While we believe we are directly voting for the president, we are merely selecting electors who then make the definitive choice. Why should a select few, amidst millions, dictate the presidential outcome, while the rest of us passively await their decision? It seems unjust that their opinions hold disproportionate weight in choosing the nation's leader.

Conversely, a popular vote system allows every citizen to directly vote for the President and their chosen team. This empowers us to express our preference for leadership and, ideally, effect positive change. Even if one's preferred candidate is unsuccessful, their vote is still counted and contributes to the overall expression of the nation's will. While the Electoral College may offer a swifter resolution, the knowledge that one's vote genuinely matters could galvanize greater citizen engagement and thoughtful consideration of candidates. It enables us to assert our preferences, rather than hoping for alignment with an elector.

I acknowledge the Electoral College's merits, such as ensuring a decisive election outcome. Some might argue that selecting electors is functionally equivalent to direct voting. However, these minor advantages pale in comparison to the fundamental importance of direct suffrage for all citizens.

In essence, when determining the presidential selection process, one must consider: How does one wish to express their voice? Do you advocate for direct participation, or delegation to an intermediary? Personally, the choice is clear: I champion the system that amplifies individual voices within our democracy.

Picture a leisurely park stroll, perhaps shared with loved ones, becoming increasingly elusive due to pervasive pollution. Our reliance on cars overshadows options like buses, walking, or carpooling. Curbing our driving habits is vital, not only due to the financial burden of car ownership, but also because it discourages alternative transportation and contributes to environmental degradation.

Car ownership brings unexpected financial burdens. Vauban, Germany, highlights this with expensive parking spots, potentially costing forty grand atop house purchases. Bogota, Columbia, penalizes driving on car-free days with a twenty-five-dollar fine, which accumulates. Paris fines even-numbered license plates one day and odd-numbered ones the next, issuing twenty-two euro fines. Gas expenses further strain budgets. While the wealthy might disregard these costs, they significantly impact families with children.

Public transportation's value is often overlooked. Imagine a world without buses, trains, or subways—attempting to drive into New York City, for instance! Bogota's car-free day pushes people to buses or walking to avoid fines. Mayor Antanas Mockus noted widespread participation despite rain. Cities like Cali and Valledupar followed suit, drawing international observers. New York's bike-sharing program facilitates easy commutes. Despite potential perceptions, public transportation is crucial for environmental protection.

Smog obscures the stars. Paris combatted its severe pollution by temporarily banning cars, achieving a sixty percent improvement in five days, though complicated by France's preference for pollution-heavy diesel fuel. Bogota's car-free day offers respite from smog, while Vauban enjoys minimal smog due to restricted car access. Despite challenges, even minor changes benefit the environment.

Vauban and Bogota exemplify how reduced driving alleviates pollution. Paris must restrict cars to combat smog. High parking costs in Vauban and fines in Bogota encourage walking or bus usage. Prioritizing reduced driving is essential due to its financial benefits, promotion of alternative transportation, and contribution to cleaner air.

Our town stands to benefit enormously if we curb our reliance on cars. Across the globe, cities—from France to Colombia—are actively encouraging alternatives to driving. Individuals report significantly less stress when utilizing diverse transportation options. Domestic studies, too, indicate a decline in car ownership. Now is the ideal moment to emulate innovative examples, such as Vauban or Bogota, and reduce our time spent behind the wheel.

Astonishingly, a German neighborhood has demonstrated the feasibility of car-free living! In her piece, Elisabeth Rosenthal highlights Vauban, Germany, as a virtually "car-free" zone. Residents overwhelmingly favor public transportation. The article emphasizes that "70% of families do not own cars, with many selling their vehicles upon relocating!" A resident shared that she felt calmer and happier without a car, free from the tensions of driving. Numerous localities aspire to replicate this model, reducing car usage for environmental reasons. Expect more "smart planning" areas to emerge. Collective action towards reduced driving can transform our city into a car-free paradise.

Bogota, Colombia, organizes a car-free day to promote alternative transportation and combat air pollution. First-time observers are struck by the transformative impact of this day. Embracing a "car-free" approach boosts physical activity and enhances the city's visual appeal. News reports highlight the proliferation of parks and sports facilities, expansive and well-maintained sidewalks, alongside trendy shops and eateries. It's not solely about cleaner air; the city becomes more enjoyable and aesthetically pleasing!

Consequently, what if we mirrored the approaches of Bogota and Vauban? Car ownership is already trending downward in our area. Fewer individuals are purchasing cars or even learning to drive. One writer noted America's waning enthusiasm for cars. Another source revealed that peak miles driven occurred some time ago and have since been decreasing. This gradual shift away from cars is largely fueled by younger generations. Increased reliance on social media, mobile devices, carpooling, and public transportation contributes to reduced driving. The automotive industry may face disruption, but many perceive this as a catalyst for improved and more efficient transportation solutions.

Essentially, driving less translates to reduced pollution, diminished stress, revitalized neighborhoods, increased physical activity, and resource conservation. Cities worldwide are actively pursuing car reduction strategies to combat pollution. By embracing a green, car-free approach, we will participate in a global movement!

Dear Senator, I'm writing to push for getting rid of the Electoral College. I truly believe the person who wins the presidency should be based only on the national popular vote. Honestly, I think the Electoral College is unfair and a major danger to what the U.S. Constitution stands for.

The Electoral College? It weakens the very core of democracy our country started on. Yes, we get to vote, but the Electoral College can totally change who we picked, which feels like a punch in the face to what the people want! It really makes you question if we're actually a democracy. Remember the 2000 election? Gore won the most votes overall, but Bush became president thanks to the Electoral College. As "The Indefensible Electoral College" points out, "Over 60% of voters prefer a direct election, not this complicated system." It's obvious: most folks in America want a simple popular vote.

Also, I strongly criticize the Electoral College because it endangers the U.S. Constitution, particularly our rights as citizens. The basic idea is that we, the citizens, get to choose our president. However, when the person with the most votes loses because of the Electoral College, it hurts democracy badly. "The Indefensible Electoral College" worries about odd election scenarios, mentioning, "Think about a state sending in two different sets of electors! It happened in Hawaii back in '60. Luckily, Nixon, who was the VP, approved only the electors for his rival." Imagine the craziness if this double-elector issue happens again and sneaks through, giving a candidate an undeserved win! The Electoral College? Just a disaster waiting to happen.

Basically, let's ditch the Electoral College! It's not fair, and it's a big threat to our U.S. Constitution. Please think about these ideas as you form your own view on this very important matter.

Globally, pollution levels are significantly impacted by transportation. Many people may not grasp the benefits of decreasing car usage. Cars release harmful gas fumes into the environment. Before acquiring a vehicle, weigh your options carefully. The advantages of owning a car pale in comparison to the negative consequences.

Cars, trucks, and buses rely on gasoline, which negatively impacts the earth. Our planet is being harmed by these fuels, which is where we live. If we continue on this trajectory, car pollution may damage the ozone layer. Then, we might not have a place to live! There is an over-reliance on transportation. We choose to drive even when walking or biking are viable options. This contributes to the deterioration of the environment. Paris implemented a ban on certain driving practices to combat pollution. According to one source, traffic volume decreased by 60 percent following five days of severe smog! While transportation can be described in a variety of ways, the term "death wish" is an apt description.

The financial implications of owning a car extend beyond the initial purchase price, encompassing numerous expenses. In Vauban, Germany, a source indicates that car ownership is contingent upon purchasing a parking spot. This spot can cost $40,000, in addition to the house. Furthermore, cars require gasoline, which is quite expensive. Insurance premiums further increase the financial burden. Driving is inherently risky, the experience can be stressful and unenjoyable.

Cars are now commonplace, influencing daily existence. Significantly reducing reliance on cars could greatly benefit our planet. Widespread car use exacerbates pollution, damaging air quality considerably.

Bogota, Colombia, initiated a "Car Free Day" that could inspire elsewhere. This day saw many Colombians walking, cycling, or using buses, relieving the city's usual traffic congestion. Despite light rain, participation remained high, with the mayor noting the weather's minimal impact. The aim is promoting alternative transport and cleaner air. However, similar measures might struggle in the U.S., where cars dominate lifestyles.

Many cities address car-related air pollution. Paris restricted driving to combat severe pollution. Initial public reaction was negative; Reuters reported nearly 4,000 fines and 27 vehicle impoundments. Yet, traffic decreased by approximately 60 percent! Despite this reduction, smog levels resembled those in densely populated Beijing. Long-term public acceptance of such regulations remains uncertain.

Limiting or banning cars could spark discontent due to mobility concerns. However, recent research reveals fewer Americans are buying cars, driving, or obtaining licenses. A Drexel University professor suggests a potentially lasting shift. Individuals who ceased driving during crises might not revert, causing car use to steadily decline in the U.S., contributing to improved air quality.

Heavy car usage notably affects global pollution levels. Reducing car dependency poses challenges and possible drawbacks. However, the potential benefits are substantial. Envision cleaner air and increased walking, biking, and skating. Curtailing car use could improve quality of life and promote environmental well-being. Reduced driving can greatly help the planet.

Whether driving less works depends on your area and effort. Globally, experiments are testing car-free or car-light towns. Heavily polluted areas often drastically reduce car use to improve air quality.

Rosenthal's "In German Suburb..." vividly portrays car-free living. The infrastructure prioritizes walking and biking. This shift is vital. Given pollution, it's a promising model for global expansion.

Severe pollution plagues some cities. Duffer's "Paris bans driving..." details Paris's smog, resulting in a driving ban. Violators faced fines and car impoundment due to public frustration. Duffer notes, "Congestion was down 60 percent...", proving the ban's effectiveness in reducing traffic.

Worldwide, programs encourage pollution-free transit. Selsky's "Carfree day..." highlights Bogota's success. Millions switched to walking, biking, or skating, even in rain. Selsky adds that Bogota feels friendlier. “Parks…sidewalks,” have been replaced, enhancing the city’s infrastructure. It is a win-win.

With rapid global changes, adapting is essential. Younger generations question the necessity of car ownership, favoring cheaper, greener alternatives. The perception of universal car ownership is declining. While not disappearing, it's less dominant.

The Electoral College? Still a big debate! Should it stay or should it go now? It's just... weird. Why have a system where we vote for someone who *then* votes for the president? If the United States wants to let citizens pick the president, why isn't the popular vote the one that wins?

When you vote for a presidential candidate, what are you really doing? Picking people who vote? Seriously? "Does the Electoral College Work?" says you're voting for "your candidate's electors." The Constitution says we can vote for our president, but how does this make sense? It feels wrong for America and the voting system we're supposed to have. To make it worse, can we trust these electors? "The Indefensible Electoral College" says, "...Can voters control whom their electors vote for? Not always." So, the candidate you like might *not* get your vote? The Electoral College might stop you from directly voting and take away your American right to have your vote matter! It doesn't make sense, so your voice should be heard for real.

Some say the Electoral College makes sure we have a president, but does it make sure we have the president that most people want? Not always. The popular vote often feels pointless, like our voices don't matter. It's like taking away an American right. The popular vote should win, not the Electoral College. Al Gore lost, even though he won the popular vote. "The Indefensible Electoral College" said, "...according to a Gallup poll in 2000, taken shortly after Al Gorethanks to the quirks of the electoral collegewon the popular vote but lost the presidency..." This shows how it makes individual votes less important. That same article says that "...over 60 percent of voters would prefer a direct election to the kind we have now." If votes *really* mattered, we should vote on whether to keep it.

Some people think the Electoral College keeps things in order, preventing voting problems. But is that really true? Every system has issues, but the Electoral College isn't the solution. The United States was founded on listening to the people's vote, and I want that to happen for real. I want to vote directly for my favorite candidate, not vote for someone else to vote.

In short, the Electoral College: unfair, wrong, unamerican. It stops us from voting for who we want and makes us vote for electors. If you believe in American values, you'll see why this system is bad. It's not a good way to handle votes; the people of America should decide!

Esteemed Senator,

Why do we even need the Electoral College? That's what folks are asking, saying it messes up what real democracy should be. They're saying we're picking electors, not the actual person we prefer. But guess what? It works! It helps us know who actually won and makes sure there's a president for all of us.

It gives us a clear winner, which is why it's still around today. Just voting based on who's popular might actually cause more problems! Richard A. Posner mentions that the victor almost always has significantly more Electoral College votes compared to popular votes. Because of this, it's less likely that we'll be arguing about the results. He's totally right; those Electoral College votes are usually bigger, which simplifies things. Also, it stops ties, like if Nixon and Clinton tied in '92. Simply put, the Electoral College gives us a more obvious winner since it usually has higher vote numbers.

On top of that, it makes the president consider everyone. Candidates need votes from every corner, not just the places where folks already like them. We don't want a president who only cares about one area and forgets about the rest! Imagine Republicans loving the South. If a Republican only worries about the South, it's not fair to everyone else. Bradford Plumer says it's not great because candidates don't bother in states they'll probably lose. But, maybe those states have already made up their minds! Why would a Democrat even try in Texas if it's always Republican? Or a Republican in California if it's always Democrat? The voters probably already decided! And those old arguments, like Plumer's, might not even be valid anymore. Things change! To really argue against the Electoral College, you need some fresh info. Just look at how it worked in recent elections! In 2004, 2008, and 2012, what people thought about it depended on what was going on then, what promises were made, and even if young people voted. So, the Electoral College ensures that the president cares about the whole country.

So, let's keep the Electoral College! It confirms who the winner is and helps the president represent all of us. Electoral College votes tend to be higher, so we know the results. Plus, it makes the president consider the entire country, not just specific regions. Senator, please keep it! It's been working well recently, so it'll likely continue to work well in the future.

In the old days, people walked a lot - for shopping, food, and family time. Why are we so into cars these days? Places everywhere are pushing for us to ditch the car habit.

From the start, cars have been a headache for the environment. Now that we're wiser, rules are popping up to cut back or stop car use. Take Vauban, Germany: residents live car-free to help the Earth, and they're mostly thrilled. As one mom, Heidrun Walter, put it, "Having a car stressed me out. Now, I'm much happier."

Paris, "The City of Love," nixed cars due to nasty air pollution. Get this: traffic jams dropped a whopping 60 percent in just five days!

Paris had the worst air in Europe, so they had to act to keep smog from taking over. The ban really helped clear the air and kick the smog to the curb.

Big cities like New York, with their narrow streets, get seriously clogged with cars. Since buildings are packed tight, walking or biking can beat driving. New York's bike program proves it. Plus, carpooling is getting trendy nationwide, where folks share rides to reduce cars. That means less pollution, less traffic, and more cash saved on gas.

Bill Ford, the head honcho at Ford, imagines a future where owning a car is a hassle. He's looking to team up with phone companies to create cities, or networks of cities, where "people walking, bikes, cars, trucks, and buses all work together to save time, save energy, cut pollution, and boost safety." We can't just ban cars everywhere - they're often easier than trains or walking - but we need to use them less. Too many cars could trash the planet with pollution. How about a car-free day everywhere, and trying other ways to get around? Biking, for example, is fantastic exercise and faster than walking, so you get fit and arrive faster!

The Electoral College keeps presidential elections super split. Some folks think it's broken, but others say it works just fine. It's like a combo of a regular vote and a vote by Congress. Past elections had issues, and things could get even messier later. A lot of people wonder why we can't just pick our president directly since we're all about democracy. We need a better way, and the Electoral College needs to go.

Here's a big problem: we technically choose electors, and they're the ones who actually pick the president. Even if the person everyone wants wins, there are still issues, like the "winner-take-all" thing. The system isn't fair to voters because of how it works, according to "The Indefensible Electoral College." Candidates skip states they know they can't win and only focus on the close races in "swing" states. So they spend all their time in some states and ignore the rest. Back in 2000, candidates didn't even bother visiting 17 states, which shows how messed up the Electoral College is.

Another issue is "the disaster factor." Bradford Plumer said we got lucky in 2000, but the system could have way worse problems. In Louisiana, some politicians almost swapped out Democratic candidates for people who were against John F. Kennedy to mess with the vote. This shows how shady the system could get if they got away with it. Other problems could be states sending different electors or a tie. Even though there are ways to fix these things, these possibilities show that we need a solid system.

Richard A. Posner wants to keep the Electoral College because he thinks it keeps things balanced. He says smaller states get more votes to make up for the bigger states. But this doesn't actually balance things; it just gives smaller states an advantage. Posner also thinks voters in swing states are more involved. But Plumer disagrees, pointing out that campaigns ignored tons of states, like Rhode Island and South Carolina. Lots of voters in big media markets didn't even see any campaign ads. The Electoral College is easy to mess with, and we should get rid of it.

I still think the Electoral College needs to go. People should be able to pick their president. Even if it's tough, the current system is obviously corrupt. Some people claim the Electoral College is fine, but that's just not true. We need to completely replace it or make some serious changes.

Dear Florida State Senator, The Electoral College is seen by some as a good method for presidential selection. However, others argue that it's unfair and too flawed to continue using. To correct these issues, we need a better system. A nation that values democracy should implement a voting process that reflects true democratic ideals.

Proponents of the Electoral College might argue that ties are infrequent, or that electors typically follow the voters' preferences. Yet, the rarity of an issue doesn't justify ignoring its potential problems. Like a small tear, the flaws in the system can worsen. Failure to reform or change the Electoral College could lead to problems that become too significant to resolve later. Electors may consider public opinion, but they can also make choices independently.

The Electoral College significantly reduces the power of individual voters. Though you cast your vote, the electors ultimately decide the presidency. The unequal distribution of electors per capita across states creates a perception of unfairness, making some votes more influential than others. This can discourage voter participation, leading people to believe their vote is insignificant. Also, candidates frequently overlook states they deem unwinnable, focusing instead on states with more electoral votes. This causes citizens to feel disconnected from the election process, potentially decreasing voter turnout.

Aside from diminishing the people's power, the Electoral College has perceived shortcomings. While uncommon, electoral ties remain possible. In such scenarios, the House of Representatives would make the final decision. This could lead to a choice that doesn't mirror the popular vote. Furthermore, it is possible to win the popular vote but still lose the presidential election due to the Electoral College, as seen with Al Gore in 2000. The amount of issues within the system makes it a poor method. Reforms must be made before the Electoral College is used in future presidential elections to avoid further issues.

The Electoral College, while functional, falls short in representing the will of the people. The electors, not the citizens, choose the president. In a nation that emphasizes popular sovereignty, our influence remains limited. A better system is needed to align with the true essence of democracy. We should speak against the overrated Electoral College and fight for a system that represents the values that America should stand for.

Automobiles are a convenient mode of transport for many individuals. However, vehicle emissions significantly degrade the environment. Luckily, reducing our dependency on vehicles presents a viable solution with multiple benefits such as curbing greenhouse gas emissions, mitigating smog, and preserving essential resources.

Firstly, reduced car usage translates directly into fewer greenhouse gases released into the atmosphere. Consider alternatives like cycling, public transportation, or even walking! Vehicle emissions contribute heavily to greenhouse gas production, which have adverse effects. Source 1 indicates that nearly half of these emissions stem from regular cars. Thus, reducing vehicle numbers directly benefits our planet's health.

Moreover, lessening car dependency actively cuts down on smog. Smog has a detrimental effect on both our environment and our well-being. Smog is considered a kind of contaminated air, and it can cause harm to people's health. A high concentration of cars makes smog conditions worse in a number of cities. According to source 2, Paris was on the verge of experiencing worse air quality than China, which is notoriously polluted. By driving less, we improve the overall air quality. To prove this point, a five-day reduction in car usage in Paris resulted in a remarkable 60% drop in smog levels. Cleaner air is a significant advantage when we decide to use less of our cars.

Lastly, cutting down on vehicle use promotes resource conservation. Source 1 posits that reduced car dependency could facilitate the construction of smaller cities. This translates to less land required for highways, shopping centers, and residential areas. Source 4 claims this would save substantial construction materials, in addition to natural resources. Essentially, reducing vehicle use enhances the lives of all people.

To summarize, a lessened reliance on cars would benefit the planet, enhance personal health, and preserve necessary resources.

Despite the numerous demands for its abolishment, the Electoral College presents more advantages than disadvantages. The Constitution deliberately established this system to balance power between Congressional selection and direct popular vote. This approach ensures nationwide representation while empowering state leaders to advocate for their constituents' interests. Though criticisms exist, the Electoral College remains a commendable method for electing the President.

The Electoral College compels presidential candidates to seek broad appeal across the nation. A candidate popular only in the South, for example, cannot win because that area lacks sufficient electoral votes. Consequently, candidates focus on winning over less supportive regions. This is beneficial because we desire a president with widespread approval, which enhances their effectiveness in office. Candidates prioritize swing states, leading to greater voter engagement and knowledge in those areas. These informed voters are ideal for determining the election's outcome.

Furthermore, the Electoral College should be maintained as it generally aligns with the popular will. Although Al Gore won the popular vote in 2000, George W. Bush won the election via the Electoral College; however, such occurrences are infrequent, with the last instance being in 1888. Electors, chosen by parties, are expected to vote for their party's candidate, which they almost always do. Switching to a popular vote system could introduce more issues than the Electoral College resolves. It's also conceivable to have a tie in the popular vote, given the total number of votes are even. Yet, this is extremely improbable and has never happened. The Electoral College also addresses scenarios where no candidate secures a majority of votes.

Critics argue the Electoral College discourages voting because voters may feel their candidate has no chance in their state, as with Republicans in California. However, every vote contributes to determining a state's electoral votes. People participate in presidential elections to demonstrate support, not solely because they believe their individual vote will decide the outcome. The Electoral College has proven effective for over a century and will continue to do so if upheld.

Want to help the planet? Easy! Less driving is the key. Get this: a European study found that cars cause about 12% of greenhouse gases. Giving up your car seems hard. But, there are plenty of options! You can carpool, get a ride with family, take a bus or train, grab a taxi, or even bike! So, the problem isn't the number of choices, but the will to save nature.

Bad news for the environment: cars! Cities are fighting back with taxes to cut car use. Take Paris. They temporarily banned driving after a bad smog event! This shows the world can improve! Beijing was as polluted as Paris until the government intervened. Bogota also bans over 7.2 million residents from driving on "Day Without Cars." If you don't follow the rules, you could get a $25 fine or even get arrested. Andrew Selsky said that this "program set to spread to other countries, millions of Colombians hiked, biked, skated, or took buses to work during this car free day". It's not just about clean air, but also about people enjoying parks together.

Less cars mean a healthier you, really! Less traffic means cleaner air. More walking means less heart disease, obesity, etc.! Car companies might not like this. Ford, Toyota, Honda, etc., could lose money if we buy fewer cars, but most people won’t give up their cars. But, less car production could help the economy by saving money on fuel. The twist is that people are driving less each year! Sociologists think this could be great for the planet, even if car companies don't like it.

Guess what? Car use is dropping! Young people aren't buying cars. A 2013 study found a 23% drop in driving among young people from 2001 to 2009. Experts aren't worried, but the drop in sales makes some think the U.S. has reached "peak driving". What will happen? Good or bad? We don't know yet. Obama’s goals to cut greenhouse gases seem to have worked. Studies now show that "Americans are buying fewer cars, driving less and getting fewer licenses as each year goes by".

Okay, Senator, this Electoral College situation is pretty wild, don't you think? Should we keep it as is, or switch to a system where the candidate with the most votes wins? I'm really leaning towards that popular vote idea. Think about it: a lot of people don't even bother voting now. Maybe half of us do, if that. Maybe that's because they feel their vote doesn't matter in states like California or Texas, where the outcome feels predetermined. Check out "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23 for more details on this. Plus, as things stand, candidates only focus on those "swing states," forgetting about everyone else.

Here's another reason to change things, Senator: "only half of eligible Americans voted in 2012," according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" section 23. I think people don't see the point if their candidate doesn't have a chance in their state. And get this: some people even tried to manipulate the Electoral College in the past. For instance, some segregationists tried to sabotage Democratic Electors to undermine Kennedy's chances—read about it in "The Indefensible Electoral College" Section 11—which shows how unstable this system can be.

I understand there are arguments for keeping the Electoral College. Some say it's a compromise the Founding Fathers came up with to make sure everyone gets a fair say, giving "qualified citizens in congress" a deciding voice, as stated in "What is the Electoral College" Section 1. Perhaps the Electoral College is beneficial as the populace still plays a role in influencing the electors' choices, and historical precedent is significant, according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 15. Ideally, electors are ethical people who'll vote for whoever won the most votes, according to "In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16. But even if the electoral votes are counted correctly, the popular vote can still be higher, even though it rarely happens—"In Defense of the Electoral College" Section 16 mentions that.

Senator, we need to talk about how unfair the winner-take-all system is. Candidates spend all their time and money in the big states, running tons of ads, and they forget about the smaller states that they think they've already won. And those "swing states" get even more attention. Consider this: "Seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't see a single campaign ad," according to "What is wrong with the Electoral College" Section 13. It shows how much they care about those big states and "swing states." It's gotta be discouraging for voters in the states everyone ignores.

In conclusion, Senator, I believe we need to switch to a popular vote system. It would encourage people to vote, knowing their vote can actually make a difference and help choose who leads their state and country. We must always remember: "Power to the People." That's what truly matters here.

It seems like cars are *everywhere* now! You rarely hear about walking or biking anymore. We're all in our cars, and it changes everything. This feels normal, but should it? Rosenthal, Duffer, and Selsky? They understand. They all want fewer cars for different reasons, but they all agree. After reading their articles, it's clear: less car use saves time, boosts businesses, saves resources, and weakens "car culture".

Traffic making you late? Walking or biking could be faster if you can! Selsky said that Colombia had a car-free day. Millions "hiked, biked, skated or took buses", which made the streets "eerily... devoid of traffic jams". Less traffic means less waiting time! Walking saves time and helps the planet. Businesses can also do better with fewer cars, as Selsky noted "new restaurants and upscale shopping districts" that appeared and old sidewalks replaced with "broad, smooth sidewalks". People walk more, see more stores, and spend more money!

Saving resources is another big reason to ditch cars. Rosenthal says cars cause "12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe... and up to 50 percent in some car-intensive areas". That is not good! We *have* to drive less because car fumes hurt the planet. Some cities even try "no-driving" days. Duffer mentioned Paris having a "partial driving ban" after "days of near-record pollution". Less pollution, less traffic, and less smog happen when we drive less! Great.

Car culture might be fading away. Studies show young Americans are driving less often. Rosenthal mentions a "23 percent" decrease in driving among young people. Maybe it's due to tickets, parents, or caring about the environment. However, Rosenthal also said experts believe that these trends will help lower "carbon emissions and the environment". Car companies are changing because of this, along with changes in commute times due to technology.

To conclude, driving less saves time, helps businesses, saves resources, and weakens car culture. Think about driving less the next time you travel. It is better for the planet.

Here's the deal: the Electoral College? It's crucial, and shouldn't change. Some call it outdated, sure, but it's still the fairest way to elect a President, period. Think about it: election outcomes are less likely to spark massive battles than if all votes were totaled. No need for a second vote, either. And crucially, it stops large states from dominating everything. Keeping the Electoral College? Essential for avoiding chaos.

Electoral College victories rarely cause the kind of intense conflict you'd see if we simply added up all individual votes. Often, candidates rack up way more electoral votes than their actual popular vote percentage would suggest. Remember Obama? He grabbed almost 62% of the electoral votes, even though only around 51% of voters picked him. Thanks to the "winner-take-all" rule in most states, figuring out who wins each state's electoral votes is simple when someone gets the most votes, even without a majority. A tie in the Electoral College? Possible, yeah, but incredibly rare, even with an even number of electoral votes total. Popular vote counting? That'd likely unleash way more headaches.

The Electoral College actually prevents the need for RunOff elections. Those happen when nobody clears the 50% mark. For example, "Nixon in 1968 and Clinton in 1992 both had only 43 percent plurality of the popular votes, while winning a majority in the Electoral College 301 and 370 Electoral Votes". The Electoral College establishes a clear victor, sparing us the stress of a second vote. Because of it, RunOffs are a non-issue.

So, bottom line: the Electoral College effectively prevents big states from wielding too much influence. I truly believe we should keep rolling with the Electoral College, just like always. While theoretical improvements might exist, the Electoral College is the best functioning system we have in place right now.

The camera on Mom’s iPhone flashes as I smile wide. The little plastic thing in my hand, like four by three inches, feels like being free. It's the kid dream: on my stomach in the kitchen, toy car close by as Mom cooks, the smell of garlic strong. I finally got my driver’s license! It’s huge for teens, but people forget the bad sides of driving. Using cars less can give us better air and make everyone healthier.

Now with my license, I hate waking up to see thick smog and having to squint to school. But, my car makes it worse. Car fumes cause smog and pollute the air with greenhouse gasses. In the U.S., cars cause about half of these emissions. Source 1. Making gas and diesel makes even more, which wastes energy and releases carbon. Making fuel for cars releases greenhouse gasses, and then the cars release even more! In AP Environmental Science, I know too many greenhouse gasses are bad. They trap sunlight, even UV rays, and then the planet gets hotter. That’s global warming. Plus, air pollution looks so bad. Paris had so much smog that they banned some cars to help the air. Cutting the number of cars for one day cleared the smog, so people could go back to normal. Source 2. Smog hurts Paris' money because tourists want to see Versailles, not be bothered by pollution.

Using fewer cars helps our bodies and minds. Vauban, in Germany, got rid of cars completely. Over half of the people who moved there sold a car, and they're happier. Heidrun Walker, a media trainer with two kids, said, "When I had a car, I was always stressed. I'm much happier now." Source 1. Walker's words show that not using cars makes your mind better. Your body gets better, too. People walk, run, bike, or skate to get around. In Bogota, Colombia, they have a "Day Without Cars" every year, so people walk around the big city. Source 3. Walking is good for your heart. If more places had a "Day Without Cars," maybe fewer people would have heart problems.

Basically, using cars less has many good things, like less air pollution and better health for both the body and mind. Next time you grab your keys to drive a short way to a friend's house, think about what you're doing. Think about your health and the planet's health, and try walking instead.

Elections are super important, and they don't happen every day! When we vote, our voices are heard, and we help shape the country's path. Picking the president should be as easy as counting the most votes; a straightforward and direct way to choose our leader. We ought to vote for who we genuinely want, without anyone meddling. The popular vote's simplicity outshines the Electoral College's complexity.

Many argue that the popular vote is fairer, since it's very simple: each person votes, and the one with the most votes wins. Candidates usually try to visit many states to campaign if they can, depending on their time. However, Bradford Plumer noted that with the Electoral College, "candidates don't spend time in states they know they have no chance of winning, focusing only on the tight races in the "swing" states." This seems wrong because every state should see and hear from the candidates, understanding their goals. Sometimes, candidates forget entire states. Plumer points out that "during the 2000 campaign, seventeen states didn't see the candidates at all, including Rhode Island and South Carolina, and voters in 25 of the largest media markets didn't get to see a single campaign ad." How can people know who to vote for without seeing the candidates and hearing their ideas?

Also, it's obvious that the popular vote is way less confusing than the Electoral College. It can really cause chaos. With the popular vote, it's a breeze. You vote, they count, and the one with the most wins. But the Electoral College? Well, Plumer says that if there's a tie, "the election would be thrown to the House of Representatives, where state delegations vote on the president. The Senate would choose the vice president. Because each state casts only one vote, the single from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would have as much say as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters." Imagine a big mess, with just one vote representing so many people! The popular vote keeps it simple: everyone votes, and most votes win. No need for representatives to butt in and possibly misrepresent us.

Basically, the popular vote is better because it's fairer. We choose who we want without interference. And it's much easier than the Electoral College. A tie could make representatives vote, and they might not even know what their people want. Voting is serious, so we should treat it right. We all should vote for our favorite, with nobody else making the call, not even representatives. Voting is super, super important.

The Electoral College, it's a voting system, and here's the deal. According to the Office of the Federal Register's explainer, "What Is the Electoral College," it starts with us picking electors. These folks then gather 'round and cast votes for the President and Vice President. Afterward, Congress tallies all the votes. Basically, we're choosing people to choose the President. It seems to work, right? So, why the fuss?

Many folks want to ditch the Electoral College for a simple popular vote. Bradford Plumer, in "Why even the best-laid defenses of the system are wrong," questions, "Can voters even rely on their electors to vote as intended?" He answers, "Not really." Okay, maybe that happens sometimes, but we're still picking electors we believe will pick the right person. Gotta trust 'em. Even if we directly voted for the President, we'd still have to trust they'll do a solid job. It's a similar idea, just with an extra step.

Richard A. Posner, in "Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President," raises a valid point. He believes the Electoral College balances things out because the Senate favors smaller states. True! Big states get less Senate power. So, with a pure popular vote, those big states gain even MORE election attention due to their larger populations.

Also, the Electoral College can prevent chaos if no one secures over half the votes. Look at 1968. Nixon got the most votes (43 percent), but no clear majority. Clinton faced something similar in 1992. However, both won the Electoral College, preventing a runoff election to decide the winner.

I say, stick with the Electoral College. It works and prevents states like Texas from overpowering smaller states like Rhode Island. Plus, it helps us avoid runoffs. Some might call it outdated, but I think the Electoral College is a good way to vote.

It's pretty clear: the electoral college? Really unfair, old, and illogical. Some argue it has merits, but that's mostly just fluff. The arguments against direct voting? Weak sauce! Bob Dole was right, even though it might sting: ditch the electoral college. The big issue? Even the best defenses of it are just plain wrong. Many fans of the electoral college miss that electors can be untrustworthy. Voting for an elector pledged to a certain president doesn't guarantee they'll actually vote that way. Think about it: Nixon, Carter, Dole, the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, and the AFL-CIO all wanted to scrap the electoral college at some point. Basically, everyone wants the most votes to win. But we're voting for electors, not directly for who we want as president. Plus, the House of Representatives decides in a tie, and their votes aren't equal!

Voting for electors isn't the same as voting for the president, plain and simple. Can you trust electors to keep their word? Nope, you can't. Do voters ever pick the wrong electors? Sure thing. This "electoral college" thing is a mess and doesn't work well at all. People think they're directly voting for the president, but that's not always the case. Here's the thing: we don't pick the electors. So, who says they'll be loyal and do what the voters want? In 1960, folks in Louisiana almost swapped out Democratic electors for anti-Kennedy ones. That would've changed everything. It's easy to cheat with this system. The electoral college is a shaky foundation for our nation. "Faithless" electors have even betrayed their party and voted for someone else. It could happen again, and it's just not honest. Don't trust the electoral college. People should get to vote directly.

If the electoral vote is tied, the House picks the president and vice president. But their process is just as bad as the electoral college. It's unfair and undemocratic. Each state gets one vote. So, Wyoming's rep, with 500,000 voters, has the same power as California's 55 reps, with 35 million voters. Does that make sense? Absolutely not! That's just the way things are. People often vote for one party for president and another for Congress. So, the House's choice might not reflect what the people truly want. And if you think ties are rare, think again: In 1968, 41,971 votes could've caused a tie. In 1976, just 5,559 votes in Ohio and 3,687 in Hawaii would've done it. A few votes can cause a mess. People say "your vote matters," but is that really true with all of this going on? It's wild the Electoral College is still around. Most people want a simple popular vote.

In short, scrapping the electoral college and voting directly for president would be way better. Voting for electors isn't the same as voting for who you want, and the House doesn't represent everyone fairly in a tie. People should get to vote fairly and not be tricked. Let the people speak! "Let us Vote!" A president who says they're "For the people" should be picked "By the people."

Too many cars are making the Earth sick, and we need to stop driving so much! It messes up the planet, our cities, and even our own lives. Places all over, like here, Central America, and Europe, are choked by cars. It's a global issue, not just a local one. Burning gas in cars is a big reason the planet is getting hotter, so why is driving less crucial?

Okay, so paragraph 10 of the "Paris bans driving due to smog" article says, "After days of near-record pollution..." So what? Paris is super polluted, almost the worst ever, and that is BAD! The famous "City of Love" is getting smog alerts, so what about every other city? Everything keeps getting bigger, more factories, and more cars. How do we fix that? It'll only make pollution worse! If we do not act now, it's going to turn into a disaster in the future. Wake up!

David Goldberg said something important in "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," in paragraph seven: "All of our development since World War II has been centered on the car..." How are cars more valuable than our health? What’s the big deal? Yeah, cars are fast, but what are we losing because of them? Forget about gas; what about our lives? Taxes are paying for roads instead of parks! Now we see malls instead of nature. Trees clean the air, but pollution is killing them! We didn't care about cars before, but now everyone is talking about smog.

But there's still hope even though we need to use cars less. Car-free days can be a simple start. Drive that day, and you get a ticket, just like in Bogota, Colombia! "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in Bogota" talks about it, and it might be working! Cali and Valledupar are giving it a shot.
