Extracting it can damage ecosystems and cause oil spills that threaten marine life. By opting for trains, buses, walking, or biking, we can reduce our reliance on gasoline. Cars consume a lot of fuel, so fewer cars would lower demand.

People claim that life without a car is simpler and less stressful, as you avoid traffic and accidents. A participant at a "Day Without Cars" event in Bogota, Colombia, noted: "'It's great to have less stress and less polluted air,' said businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza." That displays how lowering car use can decrease stress. Another person mentioned, "'When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way,' said Heidrun Walter." This resident of a car-free German town is now much happier. Lower stress is another upside of fewer cars.

Some folks only need a car if there aren't good buses or trains. Someone stated, "They rely on walking, buses, or car sharing," and "'A car is just to travel from A to B when the bus is poor.'" By avoiding unnecessary car trips, we can develop a better system that reduces car dependence and improves alternative transportation. Better bus services can decrease car use, benefiting the environment and our lifestyles.

Using cars less supports environmental health and reduces stress. One source indicated, "Cars are the second-biggest source of pollution in America." By cutting out unnecessary driving and utilizing buses or other transportation options, we can curb a significant amount of pollution in America. Less pollution, healthier lives, and lower stress levels are all benefits of reducing car use.

It's becoming clear to many people how good it is to drive less. Elisabeth Rosenthal's piece, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," highlights Vauban, Germany, where cars are largely absent. As Heidrun Walter, a Vauban resident, noted, the absence of cars makes the town livelier; the sounds are those of kids and bikes, not just engines. Life thrives in the streets, unmarred by car noise and fumes.

Suburbs stand to gain enormously from reduced car use. Rosenthal's article points out a 20-year trend of making cities denser and more pedestrian-friendly. The vision is smaller suburbs, featuring excellent public transportation and walkable access to shops, which eliminates the need to drive for every chore. Even the US EPA supports this shift away from car dependency.

One major benefit of driving less is the reduction in smog and air pollution. Robert Duffer's "Paris bans driving due to smog" details Paris's effort to curb air pollution by banning certain cars. This measure reduced traffic by 60% after five days of severe smog, bringing pollution levels near those of Beijing, China, infamous for poor air quality. By restricting car travel, Paris succeeded in lowering air pollution and traffic.

Andrew Selsky's article "Carfree day is spinning into a big hit in bogota" offers further positive evidence of reducing car usage. It covers Bogota's car-free day, which aims to encourage alternative transportation methods and reduce smog. The initiative has spurred the creation of new parks, sports facilities, improved sidewalks, new eateries, and upscale stores. Businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza finds that riding a tandem bike with his wife alleviates stress and diminishes air pollution.

In essence, the benefits of reducing car use are plentiful, including diminished smog and pollution, and more convenient access to local stores. This isn’t only good for us. In addition, it ensures our planet can sustain future generations.

The invention of the wheel paved the way for automobiles. Today, countless vehicles roll off production lines yearly, which makes travel easier for people in cities and around the world. Unfortunately, gas-powered cars release carbon dioxide. This gas damages the environment, creating problems, such as ozone depletion and acid rain. Further, too many cars in one area cause traffic congestion and other issues. Heavy debt also influences the amount of cars out there. If we aim to protect our planet, we must lower our car use and production.

Global warming is increasing due to carbon dioxide in the atmosphere. Cars contribute significantly to this form of pollution. As stated in article 1, paragraph 5, "Cars make up 12% of the greenhouse gasses in Europe... and can be up to half in the US". Decreasing car production and usage can help the Earth. Reducing global warming can help ice regions last longer while keeping animals, such as penguins and polar bears, safe.

Traffic jams can be bad because their timing is unpredictable. Passage 2, paragraph 14, mentions, "traffic went down 60% in Paris" after implementing license plate restrictions on certain days. Easier movement occurs with less traffic. A three-hour wait could become a quick twenty-minute drive. This time lets people accomplish more. Lowering car numbers and traffic congestion can also reduce accidents.

Financing a car through leasing or buying can strain your finances. Debt has existed since money was invented. Anyone can fall into debt. As said in article 4, paragraph 32, "Americans without cash couldn't get new cars, and the unemployed weren't driving to work anyway". Overspending can hurt you and the economy. Reducing car ownership debt can help the nation’s economy.

Overall, many reasons exist to reduce car usage. Gas-powered cars degrade the environment, speeding up ice melting, harming the ozone, and creating hazardous weather. Reduced traffic makes travel easier and provides people with more time. Lowering car production and leasing can help the economy. Therefore, limiting cars benefits everyone.

Okay, the Electoral College? It’s an oldie, but not everyone loves it. It’s like this: you don't pick the President directly. Instead, you pick "electors," but watch out – they might not vote how they said they would! Remember 2000? The Electoral College picked someone different from who most people wanted! Some say it stops runoffs, but that's not really a great claim.

This old thing can mess things up, like in 2000. That election was a total disaster. Getting rid of it would prevent more disasters. In 1960, some racists almost switched Democratic electors in Louisiana to vote against Kennedy. And those electors can change their minds, even if they shouldn't. Even though it rarely happens, it's a HUGE flaw.

It's not just weird; the Electoral College is unfair too. Each state gets at least one vote, so someone from Wyoming (only 500,000 people) has as much power as California's 55 representatives (35 million voters!). Some say ties never happen, but think about 1979. A few thousand votes in Ohio could've tied everything! Because it's "winner-take-all," candidates ignore many states. In 2000, some states saw no visits or ads, so voters couldn’t even support or reject anyone.

Lots of people feel left out, especially after Al Gore won the popular vote but lost in 2000. People might skip voting if they think their state is decided, like Democrats in Texas or Republicans in California. Don't think people care more about showing off than making their vote count. Like we said, every vote matters. Popular vote might mean more ties, but it's better than this old, broken system. Over 60 percent of voters dislike the Electoral College – that's a lot! We need to fix these issues before things go wrong again.

It's pretty clear: we've seen failures, close calls, big risks, and unfairness. The Electoral College fans are wrong. The 2000 election and all the angry voters show how crazy it is to keep it around. This system has been here a while, and it probably won't vanish soon. But now that more people see the problems, we can make some good changes.

Studies show that dialing back car use yields awesome results: air gets cleaner, folks find new ways to get around, and communities might even become safer places.

For starters, when fewer cars hit the road, air pollution drops, which is awesome for our health and the planet. Take Vauban, Germany, as a prime example. Research there reveals cars as major culprits in greenhouse gas emissions. In Europe, they're responsible for roughly 12% of these emissions, but in car-centric parts of the U.S., that number can skyrocket to 50%! Because of this, cities worldwide—Vauban, Paris, Bogota, and some U.S. spots included—are pushing hard to cut car emissions and create a cleaner environment. Experts widely believe this trend of driving less will stick around, which is fantastic news for carbon emissions. After all, transportation is the second-biggest pollution source in the U.S. Secondly, when we drive less, we start hunting for cooler travel alternatives. Instead of just jumping in a car, we might stroll to parks, enjoy sports, and rack up more exercise. Plus, fewer cars mean less traffic snarls. In Bogota, Colombia, they hosted a car-free day, and everyone walked, biked, skated, or used public transport. The streets were surprisingly clear! In Paris, they even banned certain cars to boost air quality, resulting in a 60% drop in traffic! During these times, public transport was free from Friday to Monday. It's clear that when we chill out on car use, good vibes spread everywhere.

Heidrun Walter, a car-free convert, says she's way happier now and has ditched the stress of driving. Likewise, Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, views scaling back on cars as a way to ease stress and purify the air. Moreover, less driving amps up safety. M. Sivak's research highlights that fewer young folks (ages 16 to 39) are snagging driver's licenses, while older generations are more likely to hold onto them. One study revealed a 23% plunge in driving among young people between 2001 and 2009. This shift in our travel habits is sparking massive improvements in various areas, which is a win-win. The number of miles driven is nearly 9% below its peak, and fewer people own cars now compared to before the economic downturn. Plus, with the evolving driving population, it appears people might not revert to their old car-dependent ways.

In short, cutting back on driving can aid the environment, provide us with additional travel options, and bolster the safety of our communities.

Ugh, commutes are the worst, right? Even if you don't drive! For like, a century, cars were *the* way to get around. But, since everyone's freaking out about air pollution, fewer folks are driving these days. If we ditched cars more often, imagine the perks: way less stress, cleaner air, and just an overall better vibe!

Think about how chill we'd be without constant traffic jams and the pressure to be punctual. We could hop on a bike, take the bus or subway, or just stroll! There's this place called Vauban, Germany, almost car-free, according to one study. People there are supposedly less stressed because everything's super walkable.

Also, cars are terrible for the environment and wreck our air. In Europe, cars contribute a whopping 12% of those global warming gases! And in some car-dependent US spots, it's a crazy 50%! Paris is a prime example of how cars can mess things up. A study says their smog was once as bad as Beijing's. They actually had to ban cars until the air improved!

Cutting back on cars isn't just good for the planet, it's good for us. Check out Bogota, Colombia, which has a car-free day every year, says another study. Turns out, it seriously reduces smog, and people dig it! Even when it rains, people flock to bike races and other events. It worked so well that two other Colombian cities are trying it out. Bogota boasts the most bike paths in Latin America – a sweet 118 miles! It's not just the government pushing for fewer cars, regular people are getting in on it. According to one source, driving is down 9%. Maybe it's because more people are working from home online and don't need to drive as much.

So, things are shifting, maybe for the best. We're ditching our car dependency, which means less pollution, less stress, and a better life for all!

In the Westminster system, parliament's majority chooses the leader. But in a popular vote, the whole country gets a say. Unlike Westminster, popular votes allow for varied voting methods. Because persuasive but bad leaders exist, stopping power misuse in nationwide votes is important. To defend freedom and guarantee people's rule, America needs the Electoral College.

America is history's oldest enduring republic because its founders were wise. The Electoral College drew inspiration from both the British system and, less acknowledged due to prejudice, the Iroquois Confederacy. Echoing England, where parliament selects the Prime Minister, the Founders sought a method for leader selection. They feared a directly elected leader might threaten democracy, making the President answerable to Congress, which can impeach and remove them for misconduct. The Electoral College serves as a bulwark against dictators. History highlights its significance: Hitler gained power without election, and Mussolini rose to power due to popularity. The Electoral College, comprised of ordinary citizens, prevents such scenarios. Though not perfect democracy, it embodies representative democracy. Richard A. Posner argued the Electoral College is undemocratic in the modern sense; electors, not the populace, choose the president. Governments are susceptible to flaws. Despite the U.S. government's generally well-intentioned actions, some seek the Electoral College's abolition. Even Nixon, who resigned amidst scandal, once advocated for its removal. Critics like Bradford Plumer deem it unfair and outdated. However, the Electoral College functions as an election watchdog, ensuring fairness and providing a final vote through representatives of states and their populations, embodying representational voting.

The Electoral College represents the populace and was thoughtfully established by the Founders. Despite debate, many esteemed presidents—Kennedy, Roosevelt, Lincoln, Washington, and Jefferson—were elected through it. To secure freedom from tyranny and preserve the people's authority, the U.S. must retain the Electoral College.

Senator, though it may have had its time, this electoral college? I'm calling it old news. Americans ought to pick their own president, fair and square. What we've got now? It ain't just unfair, it's downright crooked, plain wrong. Yeah, life's tough, but does that mean we shouldn't aim for a fair country, 'specially with the debt we're swimming in? And frankly, that debt? Obama didn't help. Unequal votes because of where you live? Just not right.

When electors hold all the cards, it shrinks Americans. We vote for them, sure, but they thumb their noses at the people's will. Not only do they go against what we want, but it chips away at our faith in government. If we're voting, shouldn't we also get a say on the electoral college itself? Bet lots of folks feel the same. Maybe I'm just a kid, but hear what the country's shouting, will ya?

Plumer had it right: we're not really voting for president directly. Remember 2000? Gore won more votes, but lost the election. If everyone's equal, how come electors get the VIP treatment? It's like we're being conned into thinking our votes matter when they don't. If electors are picking the president, then why even have us vote?

Folks say the electoral college stops one group from hogging all the power, but that's not how it shakes out. Winner-take-all in each state, that's what decides things. Who are these electors anyway, better than the rest of us? I'm young, sure, but I'm as sharp as my folks. Why ignore their votes? I'm calling it: America's built on a lie, not fair. We're told we're in charge, but the elected folks and the electoral college are really calling the shots.

America's slipping, and it'll keep on slipping unless we shake things up. Ditching the electoral college? It'd help us get back on track. Why listen to a teen? Because I know what it's like to be brushed aside, even if you're capable. America knows what's best for her people. A handful of folks shouldn't decide for us all. Let us vote for president straight up, just once. If it bombs, go back to the old ways. But hey, if we don't try, we're stuck in the mud.

While a car might seem like no big deal to most, it's actually quite significant. Despite the daily prevalence of vehicles, countless individuals lack access to a car or driving experience. In Vauban, Germany, a unique decision was made to exclude cars. Aside from the train to Freiburg and roads near the perimeter, Vauban is car-free. Car ownership is allowed, but parking is extremely limited and expensive, requiring a parking spot purchase in a distant garage for a whopping 40,000, in addition to the property cost! Consequently, 70% of Vauban residents do not own cars, with 57% having sold their cars upon moving there. According to media trainer and mother Heidrun Walter, "When I had a car I was always tense. I'm much happier this way." Vauban, established in 2006, illustrates a growing trend in Europe, the US, and elsewhere: separating suburban living from car dependency, a vital element of "smart planning."

Elisabeth Rosenthal emphasized the dual nature of cars in her article, "In German Suburb, Life Goes On Without Cars," stating that while beneficial, they also contribute to air pollution and environmental damage. In Paris, severe air quality prompted temporary driving restrictions. On one Monday, vehicles with even-numbered plates were prohibited, subject to a 31 fine, with odd-numbered plates potentially restricted the following day. The elevated pollution levels were exacerbated by temperature inversions, trapping vehicular emissions. Remarkably, traffic volume decreased by approximately 60% within just five days! The polluted air rivaled that of Beijing, China, notorious for its smog. Air quality improved immediately upon the initial restriction, leading to the lifting of restrictions for odd-numbered plates the subsequent day.

Bogota, Colombia, similarly experimented with a car-free day, advocating walking, biking, skating, or bus usage. The streets were devoid of vehicles, resulting in zero accidents! This initiative was not a one-time event but rather the third consecutive annual occurrence. An estimated 7 million residents were expected to utilize alternative transportation methods. Despite frequent rainfall, participation remained high. Mayor Antanas Mockus commented, "The rain hasn't stopped people from participating." Many participants reported reduced stress and improved air quality. Businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza, cycling with his wife, remarked, "It's a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution."

In the US, car ownership and frequent driving are pervasive. Researchers now question whether peak driving has been reached. Given its vastness and suburban sprawl, the US has historically been car-centric, being the birthplace of the Model T, the home of Detroit, and the inspiration behind "Mustang Sally." However, American car affection may be waning. By April 2013, driving had decreased by almost 8%, reaching levels comparable to January 1995. Economic downturns exacerbated this trend due to affordability issues and reduced commuting needs. University of Michigan professor Michael Sivak noted that "rates of car ownership per household and per person started to come down two to three years before the downturn," suggesting that "something more fundamental is going on." Alternative transportation options are crucial to prevent a return to excessive driving. New York's bike-sharing programs, increased tolls, and widespread car-sharing exemplify evolving attitudes. A prior study indicated a 23% reduction in driving among young people between 2001 and 2009.

Diverse motivations drive the desire to reduce car usage, including concerns about safety, cost, and environmental impact.

Most people drive a lot each week. I go to school, sports, and friends' houses in my car. Some say driving is good because it helps us travel and do things. But driving too much? That's bad. It might seem hard to drive less, and some may not think it matters. But driving less is great! The air gets cleaner, we get healthier, and we're happier. That's why leaders want us to drive less.

Robert Duffer said in 2014 that Beijing has awful air because of cars. (source 2 paragraph 14) Paris had bad air, too. So, they made a rule: cars could only drive on certain days, based on their license plates. Break the rule? You paid a fine! (source 2 paragraphs 10-19) This helped Paris's air get better fast. So, they stopped the rule after one day. See? Driving less is good! Elizabeth Rosenthal said in 2009 that cars cause lots of pollution in Europe and the US. (source 1 paragraph 5) We need to drive less for cleaner air and less gas.

Driving to work might seem easy. But driving less? It can make us happier and healthier! Rosenthal wrote about Vauban, Germany. Most families there don't even own a car! (source 1 paragraph 3) Heidrum Walter lives in Vauban without a car. She says she feels less stressed now! (source 1 paragraph 3) In Bogota, Columbia, Andrew Selsky said they banned cars for a day in 2002. (source 3 paragraph 21) Carlos Arturo Plaza in Bogota said it was nice to relax and have less pollution. These people show that driving less makes us feel better. So, let's drive less!

Driving less is so great that lots of people want us to do it! The EPA supports communities where people drive less. Leaders are doing things to help. (source 1 paragraph 9) David Goldberg said we've built everything around cars since World War II. But that needs to change. Mimi Sheller said things are changing, and we're seeing a big shift! (source 4 paragraph 35) This support shows driving less has many benefits. So, we should drive less!

The world is changing! Young people are driving less now. (source 4 paragraph 41) Now is the perfect time to stop driving and use buses, bikes, or trains. Drive less, and we help keep the air clean. We also become happier and healthier! That's why the EPA wants us to drive less. We need to act now to protect the future. Driving less has so many good things. So, let's drive less!

Hey Florida Senator, listen closely! I am just a student, and not that smart when it comes to politics. However, I need to tell you I want the Electoral College to stay. This older way of doing things makes picking a president a lot easier and faster, right?

So, in the Electoral College, there are 538 electors. A candidate has to have 270 votes to win. Some people complain that we don't vote for the president ourselves, but for the electors who want them. That is true, but voters also want their plans. We vote for a candidate's promise. This system makes winning those swing states important. I think that is important. The article "In Defense of the Electoral College: Five reasons to keep our despised method of choosing the President" said that swing states are more involved in the election. This will stop repeated elections from happening, because smaller states will not matter.

Many states use "winner-take-all", where all the state's electoral votes go to who wins the popular vote there. Other states might use something called "proportional representation." I think the "winner-take-all" system is best. While other things about the Electoral College might be weird, this part makes it easier to give all of a state's votes to one person, which keeps the state peaceful. Proportional representation does not do that. This makes the presidential election faster.

Finally, the Electoral College has been around for a while. In "What Is the Electoral College?", it said "The founding fathers established it in the Constitution as a compromise between election of the President by a vote in Congress and election of the President by a popular vote of qualified citizens." This part of our history matters a lot. Thank you for reading what I had to say.

Respected State Senator,

I think it's time we ditched the Electoral College and let the people pick the president through a straight-up popular vote. We should all get a say in choosing someone who can actually improve things for us. The current system, where we vote for electors who then choose the president, just doesn't feel right. Presidential elections should be inclusive, not exclusive. We need to overhaul our voting process.

Consider this: why should folks have to vote for electors who then vote for the president? "The Indefensible Electoral College" (source 2) nails it by saying the Electoral College is rigged because candidates only care about states they might barely win. On the flip side, "In Defense of the Electoral College" (source 3) admits that no single region has enough electoral votes to win on its own. This highlights how the Electoral College screws over certain regions because their votes might not even matter, and the president could just ignore them.

Furthermore, everyone, not just citizens, ought to have the right to vote. When tons of people are excluded because they're not citizens, it doesn't truly reflect the will of the people. This is like how some regions are electorally disadvantaged. It creates an unfair situation where some voices are amplified, and others are muted. Source 3 points out that voters in "tossup" states are more engaged because they know their votes count. But it's not fair to limit voting to only those "thoughtful voters" when others are eager to participate too.

Now, the Electoral College isn't all bad. It simplifies voting for electors. In case of a tie, the House of Representatives steps in, with each state getting one vote. The system is already up and running and has built-in solutions. However, while popular vote might have its own challenges, we can tackle them. Plus, the Electoral College can cause some regions to lack sufficient electors.

To wrap it up, the United States should switch to electing its president by popular vote. This could reshape our society for the better, empowering everyone to participate and feel valued.

Globally, individual car usage is widespread for transportation. However, this car-centric approach presents significant challenges, negatively impacting both environmental health and individual well-being through heightened stress levels. Therefore, minimizing car dependency could yield substantial improvements.

Reduced car dependence promises cleaner air in congested urban areas. Vehicle emissions, laden with harmful pollutants originating from gasoline combustion, contribute significantly to air pollution. Analysis indicates that "passenger cars are responsible for 12% of greenhouse gas emissions in Europe and up to 50%...in the United States"Source 1, emphasizing the substantial environmental impact of automobiles. Paris, heavily reliant on cars, experienced extremely high smog levels from vehicle emissions. Reuters noted, "Paris had 147 micrograms of particulate matter per cubic matter compared with 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London"Source 2. A temporary car ban led to smog reduction, highlighting the harmful effects of car emissions on environmental quality.

Moreover, diminishing car reliance can alleviate community stress. In Vauban, Germany, a car-free policy fostered tranquility. Heidrun Walter, a resident, stated, "When I had a car I was actually tense. I'm much happier this way,"Source 1 demonstrating the peaceful atmosphere created by car-free environments. Bogota, Columbia's "Day Without Cars" led to positive changes. Andrew Selsky reported improvements like, "pitted sidewalks have been replaced by broad, smooth sidewalks" and "rushhour restrictions have dramatically cut traffic"Source 3. Reducing car use can relieve the pressures of driving and traffic congestion.

Despite their convenience, cars have downsides. Emissions contribute to climate change, and traffic congestion induces stress. Reducing or eliminating car use can promote a healthier planet.

Dear Senator, I must express my concerns regarding the Electoral College. The method we use to select presidents feels unjust. While it has merits, the drawbacks, in my opinion, overshadow them. Frankly, I find the Electoral College flawed and needing abolishment.

With 538 electors, a tie is possible, albeit hasn't occurred yet. Historically, minor vote shifts could've altered outcomes. Richard A. Posner pointed out a tie's plausibility due to 538 being even, yet rare. Oddly, only a few votes could cause a tie. In 1976, a few changed votes in Ohio and Hawaii would've tied the election. These numbers seem significant, but small in total voters. Despite each state's electors based on size, a tie remains a concern, preventable by simply counting the popular vote.

Also, electors are voters, but can cause issues. Politics can be tough. In 1960, Louisiana almost used the system to select electors against John F. Kennedy because of personal preference. Posner mentioned that parties pick electors they trust to vote for their candidate, and they usually do. Still, the electoral vote winner can lose the popular vote. Electors can, in theory, vote for whoever they want, which is unfair. Voters might feel ignored. One vote might not alter everything, but candidates must appeal to everyone. They often avoid campaigning in states they'll win, since no region alone can elect a president. This can make voters feel unimportant and avoid voting. If the most votes determined the winner, people would feel valued and be more likely to vote.

Recall the 2000 election? A fight over the popular vote erupted. Confusion about votes in a state determining electors can cause problems. When a candidate gains more votes, it helps them and hurts those who voted for the other person. Posner discusses runoff election problems. Some candidates might not get a majority of votes, complicating matters and leaving the decision to the Electoral College.

In essence, the Electoral College seems unfair because of its potential issues. The US should adopt a better, fairer presidential election method, where every vote is equal. The current system can lead to ties, broken promises, and conflicts over close votes. Shifting to a popular vote system would ensure every voice is heard. Thank you for considering this critical matter.

Senator, I'm writing to tell you why I, and many others, think the Electoral College is super important and should stay. Yeah, it has some issues, but it helps both state and national elections a lot. It's a way of voting that really shows what people want, based on everyone having a say, which makes elections stronger and more believable.

To start, regular citizens get to pick who's in the Electoral College. This means people who think and value the same things get chosen to represent the community. This boosts the chances that their votes will truly show what the people want. Also, each state's voice is based on how many people live there. These electors are often just regular folks who get the needs of the public. If they are chosen well, they will really push for what's best for their state. So, the Electoral College is a much more aimed way of voting.

Sure, some argue the Electoral College has some risks, such as electors not doing what they promised. But that's not just an issue with the Electoral College. Candidates for president do it all the time, but they don't always keep their word. The Electoral College also needs trust. Just as you trust a candidate to put the country first, you have to trust electors to do their job well. Trust is super important in any election.

Finally, the Electoral College makes the election results clearer than just counting all the votes. What if there's a tie? That would be a mess! But the Electoral College, with its system of representation, can prevent a tie and maybe help a state or party. It does involve some luck, but that's better than just flipping a coin. Basically, the Electoral College makes voting more solid.

To sum it up, we should absolutely keep the Electoral College. It is a more focused way to vote, it requires no more trust than any other election, and it makes the whole system more reliable.

The automobile has swiftly evolved into the favored choice for extended journeys. An escalating proportion of individuals rely on cars for their daily commutes. Numerous individuals are displaying indolence, opting to eschew bicycles or roller skates for brief distances. In lieu of ambulating to the local convenience store, a greater number of individuals are resorting to vehicular transport. This heightened utilization of automobiles precipitates considerable atmospheric contamination, thereby engendering detrimental health ramifications and an escalation in global temperatures. In essence, curtailing the reliance on automobiles constitutes an advantageous resolution.

If we diminish our car dependency, perhaps through regulating individual driving frequency, we have the opportunity to substantially purify the atmosphere and invigorate physical activity among the populace. Vauban, situated near France and Switzerland, pioneered a car-free initiative, thereby prohibiting street parking, private driveways, and residential garages. Consequently, the majority of families residing therein abstain from car ownership, with a significant proportion having divested their vehicles prior to relocation. As quoted by a resident, Heindrun Walter, contentment is derived from the absence of vehicular transport, reveling in the tranquility of streets frequented by cyclists and playing children, unmarred by the cacophony of automobiles.

Paris, widely recognized for its pervasive smog, has additionally undertaken measures to curtail vehicular usage. "As a result of continuous days marked by pronounced pollution, Paris instituted specific driving prohibitions with the objective of ameliorating air quality." Because of these regulations, "Traffic congestion experienced a noteworthy reduction in Paris, following a five-day episode of intense smog..." Through the diminution of vehicular prevalence, individuals are compelled to explore alternative modes of transportation, encompassing cycling or pedestrianism. This fosters enhanced physical activity and mitigates the potential for grave ailments, such as oncological disorders, diabetes mellitus, and respiratory or cardiovascular dysfunctions. Diminished car usage holds the potential to rectify or impede global warming, thereby safeguarding against marine inundation and ensuring the safeguarding of the stratospheric ozone layer.

"BOGOTA, ColombiaIn accordance with a burgeoning initiative, millions of Colombian citizens partook in pedestrianism, cycling, roller skating, or availed themselves of public transportation en route to their respective workplaces on a car-free day, thereby rendering urban thoroughfares devoid of vehicular congestion." These pleasurable activities serve as a conduit for achieving one's destination while concurrently engaging in physical exertion. As proclaimed by businessman Carlos Arturo Plaza, while cycling alongside his spouse, this endeavor affords a respite and contributes to the purification of the atmosphere. As articulated by Mimi Sheller, a professor, the prevailing circumstances denote a protracted transformation, signaling a progressive shift in societal dynamics. The global landscape is undergoing transformation, consequently impinging upon our health and environmental well-being.

Diminishing the extent of vehicular usage has the potential to engender more pristine atmospheric conditions and foster heightened individual well-being. Should we curtail the production and utilization of automobiles, individuals are poised to embrace alternative modes of transportation, encompassing cycling, roller skating, or ambulation. The juncture for instigating this metamorphosis is imminent. Numerous urban centers have initiated this transition, and it behooves all entities to partake in this endeavor, one incremental progression at a time.

In American politics, the Electoral College is still a hot topic of debate. This method determines who will be the next president. Many people -- some studies indicate around 60% -- would like to abolish it and adopt a simple popular vote system instead. But there are staunch defenders of the Electoral College. Due to the design of the Electoral College, a candidate can secure the presidency without actually winning the most individual votes. So, a popular vote should take its place.

Every state gets a specific allocation of electoral votes. Each begins with two, then gains extra based on population size - varying from three to as many as fifty-five, as different sources confirm. In order to win, a candidate has to secure 270 of the 538 electoral votes available. Generally, the person who secures the greatest number of votes in a state receives all of that state's electoral votes. Maine and Nebraska stand out as exceptions. Some people feel their voice counts for nothing, so they do not bother showing up at the ballot box, particularly if they identify with a political party that is unsuccessful in their state because of the rules of the college. Those who support the Electoral College argue that it makes sure there is always a clear victor. It also keeps someone from winning by catering only to a single area of the nation. Furthermore, it is capable of ensuring a result even if the popular vote ends up in a virtual tie.

Initially, the Electoral College's purpose was to protect smaller states. At this moment, it has mostly lost that function. Presidential campaigns are largely concentrated on swing states in an effort to secure their electoral votes. Candidates largely ignore states that consistently vote for one party because they already know the likely result. If the system fails to function as intended, why should we stick with it?

Essentially, the Electoral College is dated and not the best system for picking presidents. Although it usually chooses a winner, a deadlock is still possible. Some argue that concentrating on swing states encourages voters to be more informed and helps them reach wiser decisions. They believe those in swing states are more knowledgeable due to their votes having greater significance. However, if we eliminate the Electoral College, candidates would simply turn their attention elsewhere, thereby ending the swing states' unique advantage. The idea that some votes hold greater value than others is problematic. Thus, abolishing the Electoral College is ultimately the wisest course of action.

To the Esteemed Senator:

Debates surrounding the Electoral College's purpose are widespread. From citizens to politicians, its impact is felt. This institution elects our president while ensuring the impact of each vote. In establishing our democracy, we vowed to maintain equilibrium between the public and our government. This stability is undermined by removing the Electoral College. Retaining government effectiveness and upholding citizen voices both rely heavily on it.

The Electoral College offers unforeseen national benefits. Success requires engagement with a wide range of citizens across diverse regions. As noted in Source 3, line 19, "appeal to different regions" is required. Reliance on concentrated popularity fails. To prevent regional dominance, equity is required. This also makes sure voters understand what they are voting for and its significance.

Equality is the bedrock of our national strength. Preserving the Electoral College signifies our dedication to optimality. Source 1, line 1 states, "it's a process...the founders made it up to find a middle ground." It was formed to balance congressional and popular presidential elections. The need for equilibrium is seen here.

Furthermore, the Electoral College typically ensures the victor is indeed qualified based on the collected votes. Source 3, line 22 argues that, "it stops elections where no one gets most of the votes.” This streamlined and logical method of selecting our President is important. Despite complaints about the "electors" eroding representation, this claim is debatable, and refutable. How can people effectively vote without substantial backing?

In summary, the Electoral College is crucial for national balance. Our people rely on this equilibrium. Eliminating it would harm our nation, its future, and its people.

We should scrap the Electoral College, honestly. It complicates matters far beyond a simple national vote tally. Let's move to a straightforward system: most votes win! This whole Electoral College charade is outdated. When states begin hand-picking electors, ignoring the popular will, alarms bells should ring. Yes, there are arguments for the Electoral College, but the negatives vastly outweigh the positives.

The core issue: it's convoluted. We don't directly vote for the President; we elect electors who then choose. If a slate of electors wins a state, all that state's electoral votes go to their candidate. Picture this: a candidate favored by the winning group in Florida snags all 29 electoral votes. But issues arise, especially in tight races. A tie sends it to the House, where each state gets a single vote. It's this drawn-out saga that makes the Electoral College a problem. Just count all the votes, and we'd be done in days, not months.

The Electoral College shortchanges voters. Most states award all their votes to the winner, so candidates hyper-focus on swing states, neglecting the rest. Why put people through this when a simple count suffices? Ditching the Electoral College simplifies elections, fostering national calm by reducing political squabbles.

To be frank, the Electoral College feels unfair, archaic, and illogical. Arguments for keeping it are weak, while objections to a popular vote are even weaker. The risks of maintaining the Electoral College are substantial. Shifting to a popular vote simplifies presidential selection and, dare I say, makes the nation a bit happier with fewer political hysterics.

To the State Senate, I am writing to strongly advocate for a significant shift: moving from the Electoral College to a simple popular vote for presidential elections. After examining the Electoral College's intricacies and various viewpoints, I've become convinced that maintaining the status quo is untenable, and a popular vote is the superior alternative.

The Electoral College presents a complex challenge. As Posner notes, it deviates from direct democracy. Rather, "electors elect the president, not the people... you're actually voting for a slate of electors." Hence, your vote goes to a group committed to a candidate. Reinforcing this, the Office of the Federal Register clarifies, "The Electoral College is a process, not a place," emphasizing its procedural nature.

Plumer raises a disturbing possibility: "Perhaps most worrying is the prospect of a tie in the electoral vote." Should this occur, the House of Representatives determines the winner, but with a catch: "Because each state casts only one vote, the single representative from Wyoming, representing 500,000 voters, would have as much say as the 55 representatives from California, who represent 35 million voters." Thus, a smaller state wields equal power to a much larger one, highlighting inherent inequity. The 2000 election, where Gore won the popular vote but Bush secured the presidency via the Electoral College, serves as a stark example. Plumer contends "...the electoral college is unfair to voters...swing states..." Candidates concentrate solely on select states, thus, Plumer concludes, "It's official: The electoral college is unfair, outdated, and irrational," due to potential ties, unfair representation, and systemic flaws.

Proponents defend the Electoral College, citing "...Certainty of Outcome...Everyone's President...Swing States...Big States... Avoid RunOff Elections..." (Posner), however, these arguments are weak. The 2000 election outcome was uncertain. "Everyone's President" is false since electors vote, not us. Swing states benefit at the expense of others. Posner argues it aids big states, stating that "The Electoral College restores some of the weight in the political balance that large states by population lose by virtue of the malapportionment of the Senate decreed in the Constitution..." But, this does not resolve the inequity. And, although Posner thinks the Electoral College "avoids that problem of elections in which no candidate receives a majority of the votes cast," the weight of votes is unequal, a glaring product of the swing state phenomenon.

In conclusion, replacing the Electoral College with a popular vote is essential. The existing system is demonstrably flawed, as evidenced by the 2000 election. The outsized influence of swing states and the nightmare scenario of electoral ties further underscore its deficiencies. Its inherent bias, irrationality, and unfairness are undeniable. Although some may disagree, the case for a just, democratic popular vote is overwhelming.

Car ownership is common for American adults. Many see them as essential for transportation. However, alternatives exist, along with options to reduce car usage, which is gaining traction. Although challenges remain, the benefits are significant.

Worldwide pollution is a major concern, and cars play a substantial role, affecting health and the environment. Paris attempted to restrict certain vehicles to combat smog. Amazingly, Source 2 states that after just five days of smog, Paris saw a 60 percent drop in traffic! A single day of reduced car usage made a noticeable impact. Imagine the improvement with sustained reductions over weeks or months. Complete car abandonment isn't necessary; mindful usage is key.

Reducing car dependence can promote relaxation and well-being. It may seem counterintuitive, but alternative transportation can be less stressful. Vauban, Germany, virtually eliminated parking, driveways, and garages, positioning shops within walking distance. Heidrun Walter, a media coach and mother, stated in Source 1, "I was always stressed with a car. I'm way happier now." If a busy parent can manage, many others can too. Constant indoor confinement with family can cause tension. Making walking more convenient and affordable than parking encourages outdoor activity, socializing, and fresh air—an appealing contrast to constant car confinement.

Undoubtedly, less driving is advantageous, and personally, I support reducing car use. However, widespread adoption hinges on improving accessibility to destinations typically requiring a vehicle.

Automobiles, for a considerable duration, have served as the predominant mode of transit for individuals. Nonetheless, their ubiquitous presence has engendered a multitude of problems. A strategic reduction in reliance on cars could prove significantly beneficial. This shift would encourage increased physical activity, mitigate environmental pollution, and facilitate financial savings for both individuals and urban centers.

An advantageous consequence of diminished car usage lies in its promotion of physical activity. Our reliance on vehicles often leads to sedentary lifestyles. Cars have supplanted walking, cycling, and skating, relegating these activities primarily to children. In locales such as Vauban, Germany, where car ownership is minimal, the sounds of children playing and bicycles whizzing by permeate the air. This phenomenon underscores the correlation between reduced car dependency and heightened physical engagement. Furthermore, Bogota, Columbia's "Day Without Cars" initiative yielded remarkable success, culminating in the creation of over 118 miles of bike paths within three years. Consequently, curtailing car usage demonstrably fosters a more active populace.

Moreover, a decrease in car usage would substantially curtail environmental pollution. The sheer volume of automobiles worldwide contributes significantly to global pollution levels. In Europe, cars account for approximately 12 percent of greenhouse gas emissions, whereas in the United States, this figure approaches 50 percent. This pollution, particularly concentrated in densely populated urban areas, deteriorates air quality. Indeed, few sources rival cars in terms of pollution output, with power plants representing a notable exception. Therefore, lessening car usage will benefit the environment by decreasing pollution. Ultimately, cars are a large source of pollution, and their reduction would benefit the planet.

Finally, reduced car usage would yield cost savings for individuals and municipalities alike. The maintenance, fuel expenses, and general upkeep of automobiles impose substantial financial burdens on car owners. In April 2013, driving rates decreased by 9 percent, reverting to levels observed in 1995. This shift was attributed to financial constraints preventing many Americans from purchasing new vehicles. Car ownership, thus, represents a considerable expense for average citizens. Moreover, cities allocate significant funds to transportation infrastructure, with a disproportionate emphasis on highways (80 percent) in the six-year transportation bill, while other modes of transport receive comparatively little funding (20 percent), raising concerns among lawmakers. So, driving less is helpful because cars have large costs for everyone.

Picture this: a world without cars. Streets are totally empty, no traffic jams, quiet—just peace and space. A lot of us would see that as a much nicer place to live. And honestly, driving less would really help the planet. Less of those gas-guzzling, speeding cars on the road could be great for the Earth. It would save us money, and make everything safer.

It's awesome that car companies are trying to make hybrid cars and cars that don't trash the planet as much. Still, the biggest thing we can do is have way fewer cars out there. Studies show greenhouse gases can drop "...up to 50 percent in some car-heavy spots in the United States." Plus, there's just more pollution, carbon, and gross smog. Shoot, Paris even had to ban driving for a while to deal with the smog! "...Last week Paris had 147 micrograms of PM per cubic meter compared with 114 in Brussels and 79.7 in London...". These numbers are getting scary in Europe and the US. Imagine how awful it could get in twenty years if this keeps happening. Ditching a bunch of cars could seriously drop those bad levels. It'd stop gases from ruining the environment and clean up the gross air.

Everybody loves saving a buck, right? Think of saving thousands every year. If you drive less, you could save loads of cash by hopping on the bus, skipping car insurance, and not owning a car. Some folks think not having a car is tough. After all, they need to get around. But in Vauban, Germany, cars are pretty rare, and people are doing just fine. Reports say "Vauban, home to 5,500 residents within a rectangular square mile may be the most advanced experiment in lowcar suburban life". They can walk, bike, or hop on the tram. Now, lots of American towns have carpool apps, buses, and van services. Instead of blowing cash on gas, car payments, and insurance, try sharing a ride or getting some exercise!

When you see someone drive off, do you ever worry if they'll be okay? Stats show car deaths and injuries happen way more than accidents with other ways to get around. Less cars means less drunk driving, fewer crashes, and fewer people getting hurt or worse. It's all too common to hear about teens driving drunk and causing horrible accidents. If we use buses and trains, we can stop these crashes and keep kids safer. Plus, traffic makes people stressed and angry, leading to more wrecks because they drive like maniacs. Walking instead of driving can help, and you get fresh air and exercise.

So, cars are a mixed bag. They help us get around, but they mess up the planet, drain our wallets, and aren't always safe. If we drive less, we help the environment by cutting greenhouse gases, pollution, and carbon. We also save tons of dough by skipping car costs. Fewer cars save lives by stopping drunk driving and crashes, which means fewer deaths and injuries. Next time you're walking, think about what it would be like if there were no cars.

Across the globe, locales are aiming to curtail vehicular dependency. Some are implementing "no-car" zones or restricting car usage. There are even "Car-Free Days!" The primary motive? Tackling pollution, particularly emissions from diesel trucks. This concept is gaining traction in places such as Vauban, Germany; Paris, France; and Bogotá, Colombia. Positive results are emerging, inspiring hopes for wider adoption!

In Vauban, Germany, most residents don't have cars. Street parking or car-related things are banned in this "new test area." Car ownership is allowed on the town's edge, but parking is very tight. For parking, you need to buy a spot in a garage far away. It might be $40,000!

It turns out people love living without cars in this spot. They like hearing "bikes and kids playing" better than cars. Vauban is a prime example of "smart planning." When finished in 2006, most new residents sold their cars!

Vauban shows how Germany is trying for 20 years to make suburbs that help people walk, bike, or take the bus. Around 5,500 people live in just one square mile there. It's one of the best examples of a car-free suburb. Germany wants it easier to move around without a car. Stores are on the main streets near houses instead of far away malls you get to on the highway.

Though Germany seeks car-free towns, some still want big suburbs with garages like in New York. While some still like 1950s' ideas, many places are starting to copy Vauban.

Paris, France, stopped some people from driving to fight bad smog as bad as Beijing, China! The city told drivers with even license plates to not drive. If they did, the cost was 22 euros ($31 USD). The next day, drivers with odd-number plates could not drive, too.

Almost 4,000 cars were towed for breaking the rule! But traffic fell almost 60%! French leaders said diesel cars were the issue, even with a tax that cut diesel's cost over gas. So, 67% of cars in France are diesel.

Paris has bad air in Europe from so many diesel cars. The driving ban hurt, like delivery companies losing money. But there were good things! Electric cars, hybrids, and cars with three or more people drove. Public transit was free, too. The air cleared up, and the ban was gone after a few days.

Bogotá, Colombia, started "Car-Free Day" and hoped others would do it, too! Millions walked, skated, biked, or took the bus to work that day. Colombia has had this Car-Free Day for one day a year for three years. If you drive that day, it costs $25. In a city with 7 million people, they want less smog, and people to use other ways to get to work, school, and home.

Even with rain, more people came than they thought. Two more cities in Colombia joined for the first time, too! Officials from other countries looked at what happened in Bogotá. They were excited and said Colombians were changing and said this change would go everywhere!

This idea is up since the mid-1990s. Bogotá built 118 miles of bike paths, more than any Latin American city. They also built lots of parks and sports spaces. Small sidewalks were replaced with wide, smooth paths to walk on. New restaurants and stores opened because of Car-Free Day.

Studies say Americans are buying less cars and driving less. This fits goals Obama set. Since the US buys more cars than almost anyone, people may not love cars as much due to pollution.

One reason could be the recession. Many Americans couldn't afford new cars, and that keeps happening. So, big car companies have money issues.

Ford and Mercedes change what they do and call themselves "mobility companies." They make different products. Also, young people (16 to 39) get less licenses. Many share rides, walk, or take the bus. They think they do not need a license.

Many countries stop driving sometimes to fight pollution. They see cleaner air. These countries fix big issues that cause bigger problems, such as global warming. Things change, and it helps everyone!

Senator, with consideration to its past, I still think the Electoral College serves as the superior means for selecting our leaders. This is thanks to advantages that include, but are not limited to: an emphasis placed specifically on swing states, a system that allots electoral votes dependent on state size, and the impediment it provides in preventing a presidential win solely on the back of one geographical area’s support.

The Electoral College makes swing states force individuals to think more deeply. Because individuals in these states realize their votes carry significant weight, they're compelled to consider who they vote for in a more genuine manner. This ultimately increases the chance of the best candidate winning. Voters located in swing states are pushed to more thoroughly examine candidates. Without its winner-takes-all-method, a popular vote would make these swing states have less meaning.

The way the Electoral College gives votes based on how many people live in a state is another huge factor. Candidates for president really want to win in big states since they have more votes to give. A popular vote might cause candidates to focus on smaller states, and forget the big ones. Our current system keeps elections fairer by blocking states with fewer people from having too much power.

On top of everything else, the Electoral College stops someone from winning just because they're popular in one place. Candidates have to campaign everywhere, even in places where they are not already popular. If we used the popular vote, candidates would not have a clear target for their campaigns, because the winner-takes-all feature would be gone. The Electoral College makes it more difficult for a candidate with mainly regional backing to be victorious, since other regions would no longer be perceived as important.

In conclusion, I think that the Electoral College is better than the popular vote when it comes to electing our presidents, and that is for all the reasons I have just expressed.

This Electoral College? It feels ancient for picking presidents. Seriously, why *still* use it? America *must* switch; the most votes win, period.

Okay, winner-take-all *sounds* easy, but fair? The Federal Register says most states do this: win the state, get all electoral votes. Some say, work hard in a state, deserve all votes, even by a *little*. High risk, high reward, yeah? That’s the argument to keep it. Plus, article one? Each candidate has electors, *party*-chosen, who actually vote. So, we vote for *them* to vote for the candidate? Nah. People choose directly! End the Electoral College.

Bradford Plumer's "The Indefensible Electoral College" hits hard. We don't *really* vote for president, but for electors, then *they* pick the president. Every vote *must* count the same! Giving some votes more power because of who you are? Not right! Plumer says, "If you lived in Texas... you'd vote for a slate of 34 Democratic electors pledged to Kerry.” Why Kerry get 34 votes just 'cause his party picked those people? Only get votes from people who *chose* him! Don't like him? No vote. All votes equal: ditch it.

Plumer also mentions a scary tie. Then what happens? The House decides, each *state* gets one vote. Seriously? Wyoming, *small*, gets the same say as California, *millions*? Unequal! A popular vote makes ties *almost* impossible. Also, Plumer points out, "Al Gore... won the popular vote but lost the presidency." The guy most people wanted didn’t win because of this *Electoral College thing*! Bush became president. Messed up, way more problems than it solves.

Popular vote, now. Better for everyone, way fairer to candidates. The Electoral College must go, finally.

No one can deny how important cars are these days; they're practically necessities. But while cars make life smoother, the downside of having so many of them is often overlooked. This essay will investigate these effects, detailing both the issues and possible solutions.

A major concern is the impact cars have on global greenhouse gas levels. In Europe, cars account for around 12% of these emissions. Vauban, Germany, is actively addressing this issue. This unique town is aiming to be "car-free," requiring significant adjustments. This idea is gaining traction, with plans emerging for denser, smaller suburbs with good bus and train links. "With a car, I was always stressed. This is a happier life," says Heidrun Walter, a local. This shows that, though some struggle with the change, others discover improvements.

Smog, a severe issue in large cities, is another consequence of car use. Paris, overwhelmed by pollution, was forced to restrict driving to improve air quality. Diesel fuel, made cheaper by French tax laws, intensifies the smog, highlighting the need for regulatory changes. However, recent data indicate a decline in car purchases, license acquisitions, and driving habits in America. This trend could positively impact the environment, given America's significant pollution contribution. The internet is also helpful, allowing young people to connect virtually, reducing the need to drive. Bill Ford, Executive Chairman of Ford Motor Company, stated, "Pedestrian, bicycle, private cars, commercial and public transportation traffic are woven into a connected network to save time, conserve resources, lower emissions and improve safety."

Bogota, Colombia, is implementing a program to encourage alternative transportation and decrease smog. According to businessman Carlos Arturo, "It's a good opportunity to take away stress and lower air pollution." The city has increased sports facilities and parks, improved sidewalks, eased rush-hour restrictions, and introduced appealing shopping zones. Other nations are closely monitoring this project.

These are just a few of the positive results of reducing car use. These initial findings suggest that further improvements are possible if more people and communities make efforts to decrease their reliance on cars.

The way we pick the U.S. president is by having people vote. Whoever they like best gets their vote. But, sometimes, the person who gets the most votes overall does not win. Instead, we use the Electoral College. If you get the most votes in a state, you win all of that state's "electoral votes." Then, a group of people casts these votes. I don't think this system is very good, and I'm not alone. It messes up who gets heard, makes people not want to vote, and makes candidates only pay attention to some states.

One big problem with the Electoral College is that it's not fair to everyone. States with more people get more electoral votes. For example, Wyoming has only about 500,000 voters, so they don't get very many electoral votes. This means each voter's opinion is less important when you're trying to get the 270 electoral votes needed to win. That just isn't right. Also, sometimes the person with the most votes overall still loses, like when Bush beat Gore in 2000.

Some people say it's okay to give electoral votes based on how many people live in each state. But I think a national popular vote would be much fairer. That way, whoever gets the most votes nationwide wins. In states where one party is very popular, like Texas with the Republicans, that party usually wins every time. So, if you're a Democrat in Texas, you might feel like your vote doesn't matter, so you don't vote. That's not fair to those voters, and it's not really what America is all about.

When a state gets more people, it also gets more Electoral College votes. So, candidates really focus on a few "big states" to try and win them over. The other states, the ones with fewer votes, often get ignored. When candidates travel around to give speeches, some states get totally skipped. For example, in the 2000 election, candidates didn't even go to seventeen states. That makes people in those states even less likely to vote.

So, yeah, the Electoral College is not great. It doesn't represent people well, it makes them not want to vote, and it makes candidates only care about the big states. If we switched to a national popular vote, that would fix all of that and make presidential elections much better.

Respected Senator,

I am contacting you regarding the pros and cons of the Electoral College (EC). As the mechanism by which we elect our president and vice president, it inspires worry amongst quite a few US citizens. Some critics call it outdated and unjust, and suggest a simple popular vote be used. While the absence of direct power over presidential selection may be frustrating, the Electoral College has some merits. For instance, it addresses problems and smoothes voting, also it reduces the votes required for a clear victory. Furthermore, it aims to represent various regions of the United States. Nevertheless, we must question if these positives outweigh the negatives. Is simplicity a solid reason to maintain a division between the public and their president? People believe the Electoral College is like a "safety net" intended to streamline elections. However, many Americans oppose the EC's use, viewing it as a risk to the core principles of the United States. To that end, the Electoral College should not remain in its current form.

The notion of the EC acting as a voting safety net is troublesome. While logical in theory, its application often results in skewed representation. When people cast their ballots, they are voting for party electors, expected to support their respective candidate. Richard Posner, in his work "In Defense of the Electoral College," notes that the Electoral College winner may not always secure a popular vote victory. He quickly adds that it is rare. Posner is correct in that it is indeed rare. The main reason for this is that the EC largely operates on a "winner-take-all" system, where a candidate obtains all the electoral votes for a state. When looking at ethical implications, problems arise. The president should represent most people, but some amount of people will be unhappy. To represent most US citizens, they should get the most votes. A wise citizen educates themselves on each candidate and decides who is a good fit. The act of voting is them indicating that a candidate would be a good fit if elected. If more than half pick that candidate, it is logical for that candidate to be a good fit. But, the EC gives all state electoral votes to a candidate who wins the most electors in that state. So, people that would be properly represented by a popular vote might not get true representation. That gap is not good for the entire country, or even the president.

A major concern regarding the Electoral College is that it isolates United States citizens from presidential choices. In response, proponents state that "the EC removes the issues of polling every American." Polling every American allegedly has risks such as too many votes, making it hard to accurately count. But, vote accuracy is the job of the president who, even near their end, must still represent the people who voted for them by giving them the right to pick their president. Here, "practicality" just is their way of not letting US citizens vote. A popular vote risks error, but is less risky with the Electoral College because there are fewer electors. The EC intends to reflect the population, ensuring the chosen candidate is ideal for many in an area and not just in one area. Though it tries to do its best, the EC is not accurate. Posner says, "the most thoughtful voters should decide the election," and his rationale is logical. A voter who isn't educated is a danger to any nation, no matter the nation's situation. But, the US promises every citizen the right to vote. A person in power can't just say "only the educated should decide," because an educated voter can't be easily defined.

The EC's flaws are also made worse by large states. Larger states have more people, a candidate chosen via popular vote represents those people no matter their location. Smaller states argue that states should have an equal say in the Senate and EC. Each state was given two senators, no matter their size, because of the Senate. However, the Electoral College does not give equal representation to smaller states. The candidate's win in a popular vote is dependent on the population of the state. The EC also depends on the state's population. Since smaller states don't have many electoral votes, it is thought they should have equal votes in the US Senate. Sadly, the EC doesn't offer this.

Knowing where this situation comes from is key in making actual change. Accuracy should be gained if available, but aimed for if thought to be impossible. Keeping the EC as is now lacks accuracy, and representation just for electoral ease. A well-made Electoral College should think of itself as assisting, not fighting, popular votes. Finally, I must state my dissatisfaction with the EC's current status. I can understand that practical matters can't be changed, but getting the best method possible is key and needed. Senator, you were chosen due to the vote. We believed that you would defend our needs. We would like you to represent us when picking a presidential candidate. Because of what has been said, we support removing the Electoral College for a better voting system.

Without a doubt, driving the car on extended road excursions serves a purpose; nonetheless, there exist further manners to navigate while journeying locally, for instance, utilizing the bus or cycling. Significant merits arise from diminished car reliance. Indeed, vehicles proffer convenience; however, less financially burdensome modes of transport abound.

To illustrate, transit networks coalesce both communal and autonomous avenues for locomotion. These conserve resources, curtail contamination, and simply augment journeying safety. Automobiles instigate mishaps stemming from driver weariness, lapses in concentration, or a paucity of parking accommodations. Furthermore, the monetary burden of car ownership, with its attendant fuel and maintenance expenses, constitutes a substantial concern. When fiscal constraints tighten, buses or subways present a far more economical alternative. Moreover, vehicular impoundment exacerbates matters considerably. During periods of atmospheric stagnation, vehicular emissions persist, exacerbating environmental degradation. Certain individuals assert the indispensability of automobile ownership for basic mobility; however, accessible public transit belies this notion.

Additionally, diminishing car usage aids environmental preservation. Initially, thoroughfares may appear congested; however, a reduction in smog undeniably ensues when individuals curtail habitual driving. Car-free initiatives markedly illustrate the enhancements realized through decreased vehicular operation, thereby mitigating stress and purifying atmospheric conditions. These factors collectively engender substantial improvements. Parks and recreational spaces proliferate, sidewalks improve, traffic congestion during peak hours diminishes, and novel commercial and social venues materialize. Although the United States maintains a car-centric culture, recent hardships could deter individuals from immediately reverting to commuting by car, particularly given these auspicious developments.

In summary, possessing a car entails substantial responsibility, and while occasionally requisite, it remains dispensable at other times. Certainly, vehicles retain significance, but planetary preservation demands consideration as well. While cars provide ease, alternatives exist for reaching destinations affordably.

Nonstop car alarms, loud horns, and loud engines are now normal, like our life's song. So many cars are simply too much.

Seriously, we must drive less.

Imagine the good that would come from it.

Cars seem crucial, yet often they're not. Okay, maybe for long trips. However, walking ten minutes to the store? Really?

Cutting back on cars matters, truly. Each week, a family spends big on gas – like $20-$50! Driving less? It saves big on gas, letting you feed your family.

"When I had a car, I felt so stressed. I like life better now," stated one person. Folks who get rid of cars? They often seem happier.

Better air is a bonus! That means we all live healthier.

"It eases stress and betters the air," said Carlos Arturo Plaza, a businessman, biking with his wife. Folks who drive less? They're usually pleased about it.

If more saw how cool it is to drive less and tried it, the air would clear, and we'd all feel good.

One former driver said her kids "... plan summer jobs and fun around walking, the bus, or friend rides." Plus, they aren't rushing for licenses, even with cars being useful where they live. Folks even enjoy walking instead of driving!

Driving isn't bad, but driving less? It's good for you. You save cash by walking, the air betters for all, and people can focus on key stuff: work, friends, and family.

Give it a shot! Park the car and put away the keys. Walking could feel fun, a good change from driving.

Esteemed Senator, I implore you, as a student, to champion a monumental shift in our presidential elections: the abolishment of the Electoral College in favor of a national popular vote. This transition ensures each individual's vote directly sways the outcome, instead of our current convoluted, indirect method.

The problem with the current system is that citizens aren't even voting for the president directly! These electors, chosen by state parties, erode voter influence. We need a stronger say in electing our leader.

Remember when Louisiana nearly botched their elector selection back in the '60s? That near-miss showed how easily the Electoral College can be manipulated, possibly leading to complete pandemonium. Hanging onto this system practically invites the same issues.

Think about it: "winner-take-all" also distorts campaign strategies. Candidates only concentrate on those crucial swing states, bypassing countless citizens in states deemed already won or lost.

The Electoral College is an outdated process that undermines direct representation, threatens historical crises, and fosters unjust elections with its "winner-take-all" approach. Voters deserve a transparent system, a system that avoids previous errors, and rectifies the nonsensical "winner-take-all" framework.

The electoral college, what with its electors standing between us and the presidential hopefuls, well, it ain't ideal. Instead of directly choosing the president, we're picking folks to do the actual voting, and that can leave you scratching your head about what you're really voting for. Wouldn't it be simpler to just tally up everyone's votes? Because, let's face it, the electoral college? Not exactly a shining beacon of democracy.

Sure, the thought was to level the playing field for smaller states, but those big states still hog the limelight, ya know? Some states boast a mountain of electoral votes, while a bunch more are just clinging to a handful. But hey, those little guys banding together? Suddenly, they've got more clout than the big boys. And get this – sometimes a candidate wins the popularity contest but still loses the election because their fans are all huddled in specific states. See how the electoral college messes things up? Those electors calling the shots for everyone throws the whole count off balance.

Trying to wrap your head around the gap between the popular vote and those electoral votes? Good luck! It's a head-scratcher, no doubt. A candidate could kill it in California, racking up a ton of electoral votes. But if they do the same in Montana? Nada. Hardly any votes. Even if they pour the same heart and soul into both places, the electoral vote system is a sucker for whoever wins big in the states with the most folks.

And to twist the knife a little further, it's a winner-takes-all game. Candidates camp out in places like California and New York because snatching a win, even by a hair, gets them all the electoral votes. Seriously, even if it's neck and neck, like 49% to 51%, the loser walks away empty-handed. How about we divvy up those electoral votes based on how folks actually voted? Fair's fair, right?

Bottom line? The electoral college is a hot mess. A simple popular vote? Now, that would be sweet. Easy to grasp, fair as pie, and everyone could follow along without needing a decoder ring.

The current situation in our country is a cause for concern. With the environment deteriorating and financial hardships mounting, immediate action is crucial. A potential solution lies in reduced driving, which could address both issues. Less driving translates to decreased pollution, benefiting the environment, and significant cost savings for individuals, alleviating financial strain. In essence, cutting back on driving can lead to notable improvements.

Environmental damage is worsening due to excessive pollution. Robert Duffer highlights the severe smog and pollution in Paris, France, comparable to notoriously polluted cities like Beijing, China. Consequently, the French government implemented driving restrictions and penalties for violations, temporarily clearing the smog before the regulations were lifted. This situation offers a valuable lesson for heavily polluted areas: such regulations can effectively improve environmental conditions and safeguard public health.

For individuals facing financial difficulties, reducing their driving habits can be extremely beneficial. The money saved on car-related expenses can be redirected to essential needs. Elisabeth Rosenthal notes that in certain German towns, 70% of residents do not own cars due to parking scarcity and the high cost of parking spaces, sometimes as much as $40,000. Consequently, forgoing car ownership is significantly more economical. Many have sold their cars to relocate there. Rosenthal's research underscores that not owning a car can result in considerable cost savings, especially for those struggling financially.

The advantages of reduced driving are numerous. It promotes a healthier environment through cleaner air, benefiting both the planet and individual well-being. Furthermore, it leads to cost savings, which is particularly advantageous for those facing economic challenges. While some might argue that reduced driving makes commuting to work, school, or other destinations difficult, alternative transportation options such as bikes, buses, trains, and monorails exist. Consider the negative consequences of excessive driving: substantial pollution and significant expenses on fuel and car maintenance. By reducing our driving habits, we can eliminate these problems.

Respected Senator,

It's time to do away with the Electoral College. The founders made it a while ago, and it's in the Constitution, but it should be removed. We should pick the president by popular vote, where everyone's vote counts. The Electoral College isn't fair and treats states badly, so it should go. We want freedom and equality, but this system stops that.

The USA is about everyone having rights. But how is that true when people can't just vote for the president? The Office of the Federal Register says you vote for electors who vote for the president. What's the point of voting then? It's not good if electors don't do what people want. Bradford Plumer said Al Gore "won the vote but lost the presidency" in 2000 because of the Electoral College.
